(In Ezek 17:23 and Zech 11:2 the adjective is used of Lebanon’s cedars.) Another option is to take the preposition as indicating agency and interpret “mighty one” as a divine title (see Isa 33:21). One could then translate, “and Lebanon will fall by [the agency of] the Mighty One.”

1.anchor: revealed the following message during the year in which King Sargon of Assyria sent his commanding general to Ashdod, and he fought against it and captured it.
1.note: tn Heb “In the year the commanding general came to Ashdod, when Sargon king of Assyria sent him, and he fought against Ashdod and captured it.”sn This probably refers to the Assyrian campaign against Philistia in 712 or 711 b.c.

2.note: tn Heb “spoke by the hand of.”

2.anchor: Isaiah son of Amoz: “Go, remove the sackcloth from your waist and take your sandals off your feet.” He did as instructed and walked around in undergarments
2.note: tn The word used here (עָרוֹם, ’arom) sometimes means “naked,” but here it appears to mean simply “lightly dressed,” i.e., stripped to one’s undergarments. See HALOT 883 s.v. עָרוֹם. The term also occurs in vv. 3, 4.

4.anchor: so the king of Assyria will lead away the captives of Egypt and the exiles of Cush, both young and old. They will be in undergarments and barefoot, with the buttocks exposed; the Egyptians will be publicly humiliated.
4.note: tn Heb “lightly dressed and barefoot, and bare with respect to the buttocks, the nakedness of Egypt.”

5.anchor: Those who put their hope in Cush and took pride in Egypt will be afraid and embarrassed.
5.note: tn Heb “and they will be afraid and embarrassed because of Cush their hope and Egypt their beauty.”

6.note: tn Heb “in that day” (so KJV).

6.anchor: those who live on this coast
6.note: sn This probably refers to the coastal region of Philistia (cf. TEV).

1.anchor: and executed plans made long ago exactly as you decreed.
1.note: tn Heb “plans from long ago [in] faithfulness, trustworthiness.” The feminine noun אֱמוּנָה (’emunah, “faithfulness”) and masculine noun אֹמֶן (’omen, “trustworthiness”), both of which are derived from the root אָמַן (’aman), are juxtaposed to emphasize the basic idea conveyed by the synonyms. Here they describe the absolute reliability of the divine plans.

2.note: tc Some with support from the LXX emend זָרִים (zarim, “foreigners”) to זֵדִים (zedim, “the insolent”).

3.anchor: the towns of
3.note: tn The Hebrew text has a singular form, but it should be emended to a plural or eliminated altogether. The noun may have been accidentally copied from the preceding verse.

4.anchor: Though the breath of tyrants
4.note: tn Or perhaps, “the violent”; NIV, NRSV “the ruthless.”

4.anchor: is like a winter rainstorm,
4.note: tc The Hebrew text has, “like a rainstorm of a wall,” which might be interpreted to mean, “like a rainstorm battering against a wall.” The translation assumes an emendation of קִיר (qir, “wall”) to קֹר (qor, “cold, winter”; cf. Gen 8:22). See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:457, n. 6, for discussion.

5.anchor: so he causes the song of tyrants to cease.
5.note: tn The translation assumes that the verb יַעֲנֶה (ya’aneh) is a Hiphil imperfect from עָנָה (’anah, “be afflicted, humiliated”). In this context with “song” as object it means to “quiet” (see HALOT 853-54 s.v. II ענה). Some prefer to emend the form to the second person singular, so that it will agree with the second person verb earlier in the verse. BDB 776 s.v. III עָנָה Qal.1 understands the form as Qal, with “song” as subject, in which case one might translate “the song of tyrants will be silent.” An emendation of the form to a Niphal (יֵעָנֶה, ye’aneh) would yield the same translation.

6.note: tn Heb “And the Lord who commands armies [traditionally, the Lord of hosts] will make for all the nations on this mountain a banquet of meats, a banquet of wine dregs, meats filled with marrow, dregs that are filtered.”

7.anchor: the woven covering that is over all the nations;
7.note: sn The point of the imagery is unclear. Perhaps the shroud/covering referred to was associated with death in some way (see v. 8).

8.note: tn Heb “has spoken” (so NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT).

9.note: tn Heb “this [one].”

10.anchor: as a heap of straw is trampled down in
10.note: tc The marginal reading (Qere) is בְּמוֹ (bÿmo, “in”). The consonantal text (Kethib) has בְּמִי (bÿmi, “in the water of”).

11.anchor: Lord
11.note: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the Lord) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

11.anchor: will bring down Moab’s
11.note: tn Heb “his”; the referent (Moab) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

11.anchor: pride as it spreads its hands.
11.note: tn The Hebrew text has, “he will bring down his pride along with the [?] of his hands.” The meaning of אָרְבּוֹת (’arbot), which occurs only here in the OT, is unknown. Some (see BDB 70 s.v. אָרְבָּה) translate “artifice, cleverness,” relating the form to the verbal root אָרָב (’arav, “to lie in wait, ambush”), but this requires some convoluted semantic reasoning. HALOT 83 s.v. *אָרְבָּה suggests the meaning “[nimble] movements.” The translation above, which attempts to relate the form to the preceding context, is purely speculative.

12.anchor: he will bring it down, he will throw it down to the dusty ground.
12.note: tn Heb “he will bring [it] down, he will make [it] touch the ground, even to the dust.”

1.note: tn Heb “What to you, then?”

2.anchor: they did not die in battle.
2.note: sn Apparently they died from starvation during the siege that preceded the final conquest of the city. See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:409.

3.anchor: they were captured without a single arrow being shot.
3.note: tn Heb “apart from [i.e., without] a bow they were captured”; cf. NAB, NRSV “without the use of a bow.”

4.anchor: concerning the destruction of my defenseless people.”
4.note: tn Heb “the daughter of my people.” “Daughter” is here used metaphorically to express the speaker’s emotional attachment to his people, as well as their vulnerability and weakness.

5.anchor: and cry out to the hill.
5.note: sn Perhaps “the hill” refers to the temple mount.

6.note: sn A distant region in the direction of Mesopotamia; see Amos 1:5; 9:7.
tn Heb “Kir uncovers” (so NAB, NIV).

sn The Elamites and men of Kir may here symbolize a fierce army from a distant land. If this oracle anticipates a Babylonian conquest of the city (see 39:5-7), then the Elamites and men of Kir are perhaps viewed here as mercenaries in the Babylonian army. See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:410.

7.anchor: horsemen confidently took their positions
7.note: tn Heb “taking a stand, take their stand.” The infinitive absolute emphasizes the following finite verb. The translation attempts to bring out this emphasis with the adverb “confidently.”

8.anchor: for the weapons in the House of the Forest.
8.note: sn Perhaps this refers to a royal armory, or to Solomon’s “House of the Forest of Lebanon,” where weapons may have been kept (see 1 Kgs 10:16-17).

9.anchor: in the walls of the city of David;
9.note: tn Heb “the breaks of the city of David, you saw that they were many.”

10.anchor: and demolished houses so you could have material to reinforce the wall.
10.note: tn Heb “you demolished the houses to fortify the wall.”

11.anchor: you did not depend on
11.note: tn Heb “did not see.”

12.anchor: for shaved heads and sackcloth.
12.note: tn Heb “for baldness and the wearing of sackcloth.” See the note at 15:2.

13.anchor: Eat and drink, for tomorrow we die!”
13.note: tn The prophet here quotes what the fatalistic people are saying. The introductory “you say” is supplied in the translation for clarification; the concluding verb “we die” makes it clear the people are speaking. The six verbs translated as imperatives are actually infinitives absolute, functioning here as finite verbs.

14.anchor: who commands armies told me this:
14.note: tn Heb “it was revealed in my ears [by?] the Lord who commands armies [traditionally, the Lord of hosts].”

14.anchor: “Certainly this sin will not be forgiven as long as you live,”
14.note: tn Heb “Certainly this sin will not be atoned for until you die.” This does not imply that their death will bring atonement; rather it emphasizes that their sin is unpardonable. The statement has the form of an oath.

15.anchor: “Go visit this administrator, Shebna, who supervises the palace,
15.note: tn Heb “who is over the house” (so ASV); NASB “who is in charge of the royal household.”

15.anchor: and tell him:
15.note: tn The words “and tell him” are supplied in the translation for clarification.

16.anchor: Why
16.note: tn Heb “that you chisel out.”

17.anchor: He will wrap you up tightly.
17.note: tn Heb “and the one who wraps you [will] wrap.”

18.anchor: which bring disgrace to the house of your master.
18.note: sn Apparently the reference to chariots alludes to Shebna’s excessive pride, which in turn brings disgrace to the royal family.

19.anchor: you will be thrown down
19.note: tn Heb “he will throw you down.” The shift from the first to third person is peculiar and abrupt, but certainly not unprecedented in Hebrew poetry. See GKC 462 §144.p. The third person may be indefinite (“one will throw you down”), in which case the passive translation is justified.

20.anchor: “At that time
20.note: tn Or “in that day.” The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on “in the future” in 2:2.

21.anchor: I will put your robe on him, tie your belt around him, and transfer your authority to him.
21.note: tn Heb “and your dominion I will place in his hand.”

21.anchor: He will become a protector of
21.note: tn Heb “a father to.” The Hebrew term אָב (’av, “father”) is here used metaphorically of one who protects and supports those under his care and authority, like a father does his family. For another example of this metaphorical use of the word, see Job 29:16.

21.anchor: the residents of Jerusalem and of the people
21.note: tn Heb “house.”

22.anchor: I will place the key
22.note: sn This may refer to a literal insignia worn by the chief administrator. Even so, it would still symbolize the administrator’s authority to grant or exclude access to the king. See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:422.

23.anchor: I will fasten him like a peg into a solid place;
23.note: sn The metaphor depicts how secure his position will be.

23.anchor: he will bring honor and respect to his father’s family.
23.note: tn Heb “and he will become a glorious throne for the house of his father.”

24.anchor: His father’s family will gain increasing prominence because of him,
24.note: tn Heb “and all the glory of the house of his father they will hang on him.” The Lord returns to the peg metaphor of v. 23a. Eliakim’s secure position of honor will bring benefits and jobs to many others in the family.

24.anchor: including the offspring and the offshoots.
24.note: tn The precise meaning and derivation of this word are uncertain. Cf. KJV, ASV, NRSV “the issue”; CEV “relatives.”

24.anchor: All the small containers, including the bowls and all the jars will hang from this peg.’
24.note: tn Heb “all the small vessels, from the vessels that are bowls to all the vessels that are jars.” The picture is that of a single peg holding the weight of all kinds of containers hung from it.

25.anchor: “At that time,”
25.note: tn Or “In that day” (KJV).

25.anchor: who commands armies, “the peg fastened into a solid place will come loose. It will be cut off and fall, and the load hanging on it will be cut off.”
25.note: sn Eliakim’s authority, though seemingly secure, will eventually be removed, and with it his family’s prominence.

25.anchor: Indeed,
25.note: tn Or “for” (KJV, NAB, NASB, NRSV).

1.anchor: the crown of those overcome with wine.
1.note: tn Heb “ones overcome with wine.” The words “the crown of” are supplied in the translation for clarification. The syntactical relationship of the final phrase to what precedes is uncertain. הֲלוּמֵי יָיִן (halume yayin, “ones overcome with wine”) seems to correspond to שִׁכֹּרֵי אֶפְרַיִם (shikkore ’efrayim, “drunkards of Ephraim”) in line 1. The translation assumes that the phrase “the splendid crown” is to be understood in the final line as well.

2.note: tn The words “that crown” are supplied in the translation for clarification. The object of the verb is unexpressed in the Hebrew text.

2.anchor: to the ground with his hand.
2.note: tn Or “by [his] power.”

4.anchor: he grabs it and swallows it.
4.note: tn Heb “which the one seeing sees, while still it is in his hand he swallows it.”

5.anchor: At that time
5.note: tn Or “in that day” (KJV).

6.anchor: and strength to those who defend the city from attackers.
6.note: tn Heb “and [he will become] a spirit of justice for the one who sits [i.e., presides] over judgment, // and strength [for] the ones who turn back battle at the city gate.” The Lord will provide internal stability and national security.

7.anchor: they totter while making legal decisions.
7.note: tn Heb “[in] giving a decision.”

8.note: tn Heb “vomit, without a place.” For the meaning of the phrase בְּלִי מָקוֹם (bÿli maqom, “without a place”), see HALOT 133 s.v. בְּלִי.

9.anchor: Those just taken from their mother’s breast!
9.note: tn Heb “from the breasts.” The words “their mother’s” are supplied in the translation for clarification. The translation assumes that this is the prophet’s answer to the questions asked in the first half of the verse. The Lord is trying to instruct people who are “infants” morally and ethically.

10.anchor: a syllable here, a syllable there.
10.note: tn The meaning of this verse has been debated. The text has literally “indeed [or “for”] a little there, a little there” ( כִּי צַו לָצָו צַו לָצָו קַו לָקָו קַו, ki tsav latsav, tsav latsav, qav laqav, qav laqav). The present translation assumes that the repetitive syllables are gibberish that resembles baby talk (cf v. 9b) and mimics what the people will hear when foreign invaders conquer the land (v. 11). In this case זְעֵיר (zÿ’er, “a little”) refers to the short syllabic structure of the babbling (cf. CEV). Some take צַו (tsav) as a derivative of צָוָה (tsavah, “command”) and translate the first part of the statement as “command after command, command after command.” Proponents of this position (followed by many English versions) also take קַו (qav) as a noun meaning “measuring line” (see v. 17), understood here in the abstract sense of “standard” or “rule.”

11.anchor: he will speak to these people.
11.note: sn This verse alludes to the coming Assyrian invasion, when the people will hear a foreign language that sounds like gibberish to them. The Lord is the subject of the verb “will speak,” as v. 12 makes clear. He once spoke in meaningful terms, but in the coming judgment he will speak to them, as it were, through the mouth of foreign oppressors. The apparent gibberish they hear will be an outward reminder that God has decreed their defeat.

12.anchor: This is where rest can be found.”
12.note: sn This message encapsulates the Lord’s invitation to his people to find security in his protection and blessing.

13.anchor: and be injured, ensnared, and captured.
13.note: sn When divine warnings and appeals become gibberish to the spiritually insensitive, they have no guidance and are doomed to destruction.

14.note: map For location see Map5-B1; Map6-F3; Map7-E2; Map8-F2; Map10-B3; JP1-F4; JP2-F4; JP3-F4; JP4-F4.

15.anchor: we have hidden ourselves in a deceitful word.”
15.note: sn “Lie” and “deceitful word” would not be the terms used by the people. They would likely use the words “promise” and “reliable word,” but the prophet substitutes “lie” and “deceitful word” to emphasize that this treaty with death will really prove to be disappointing.

16.anchor: The one who maintains his faith will not panic.
16.note: tn Heb “will not hurry,” i.e., act in panic.

17.anchor: hail will sweep away the unreliable refuge,
17.note: tn Heb “[the] refuge, [the] lie.” See v. 15.

18.anchor: you will be overrun by it.
18.note: tn Heb “you will become a trampling place for it.”

19.anchor: it will come through during the day and the night.”
19.note: tn The words “it will come through” are supplied in the translation. The verb “will sweep by” does double duty in the parallel structure.

20.anchor: and the blanket is too narrow to wrap around oneself.
20.note: sn The bed and blanket probably symbolize their false sense of security. A bed that is too short and a blanket that is too narrow may promise rest and protection from the cold, but in the end they are useless and disappointing. In the same way, their supposed treaty with death will prove useless and disappointing.

21.anchor: his strange task.
21.note: sn God’s judgment of his own people is called “his peculiar work” and “his strange task,” because he must deal with them the way he treated their enemies in the past.

22.anchor: who commands armies, against the entire land.
22.note: tn Or “the whole earth” (KJV, ASV, NAB, NCV).

23.anchor: Be attentive and listen to what I have to say!
23.note: tn Heb “to my word”; cf. KJV, ASV, NRSV “hear my speech.”

24.anchor: Does a farmer just keep on plowing at planting time?
24.note: tn Heb “All the day does the plowman plow in order to plant?” The phrase “all the day” here has the sense of “continually, always.” See BDB 400 s.v. יוֹם.

25.anchor: and plant the wheat, barley, and grain in their designated places?
25.note: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, “place wheat [?], and barley [?], and grain in its territory.” The term שׂוֹרָה (shorah) is sometimes translated “[in] its place,” but the word is unattested elsewhere. It is probably due to dittography of the immediately following שְׂעֹרָה (sÿo’rah, “barley”). The meaning of נִסְמָן (nisman) is also uncertain. It may be due to dittography of the immediately following כֻסֶּמֶת (kussemet, “grain”).

26.anchor: he teaches him the principles of agriculture.
26.note: tn Heb “he teaches him the proper way, his God instructs him.”

27.anchor: nor is the wheel of a cart rolled over cumin seed.
27.note: sn Both of these seeds are too small to use the ordinary threshing techniques.

29.anchor: who gives supernatural guidance and imparts great wisdom.
29.note: sn Verses 23-29 emphasize that God possesses great wisdom and has established a natural order. Evidence of this can be seen in the way farmers utilize divinely imparted wisdom to grow and harvest crops. God’s dealings with his people will exhibit this same kind of wisdom and order. Judgment will be accomplished according to a divinely ordered timetable and, while severe enough, will not be excessive. Judgment must come, just as planting inevitably follows plowing. God will, as it were, thresh his people, but he will not crush them to the point where they will be of no use to him.

1.note: tn Heb “will rule according to justice.”

2.note: tn Heb “a man,” but אִישׁ (’ish) probably refers here to “each” of the officials mentioned in the previous verse.

3.anchor: and ears
3.note: tn Heb “ears that hear.”

4.anchor: The mind that acts rashly will possess discernment
4.note: tn Heb “the heart of rashness will understand knowledge”; cf. NAB “The flighty will become wise and capable.”

6.anchor: and gives the thirsty nothing to drink.
6.note: tn Heb “and the drink of the thirsty he causes to fail.”

7.anchor: even when the needy are in the right.
7.note: tn Heb “to ruin the poor with words of falsehood, even when the needy speak what is just.”

8.anchor: his honorable character gives him security.
8.note: tn Heb “and he upon honorable things stands.”

9.note: tn Or “self-confident”; NAB “overconfident.”

10.anchor: for the grape
10.note: tn Or perhaps, “olive.” See 24:13.

11.anchor: put sackcloth on your waist!
11.note: tn The imperatival forms in v. 11 are problematic. The first (חִרְדוּ, khirdu, “tremble”) is masculine plural in form, though spoken to a feminine plural addressee (שַׁאֲנַנּוֹת, sha’anannot, “complacent ones”). The four imperatival forms that follow (רְגָזָה, rÿgazah, “shake with fear”; פְּשֹׁטָה, pÿshotah, “strip off your clothes”; עֹרָה, ’orah, “expose yourselves”; and חֲגוֹרָה, khagorah, “put on”) all appear to be lengthened (so-called “emphatic”) masculine singular forms, even though they too appear to be spoken to a feminine plural addressee. GKC 131-32 §48.i suggests emending חִרְדוּ (khirdu) to חֲרָדָה (kharadah) and understanding all five imperatives as feminine plural “aramaized” forms.

12.anchor: Mourn over the field,
12.note: tc The Hebrew text has “over mourning breasts.” The reference to “breasts” would make sense in light of v. 11, which refers to the practice of women baring their breasts as a sign of sorrow (see J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah [NICOT], 1:585). However, one expects the preposition עַל (’al) to introduce the source or reason for mourning (see vv. 12b-13a) and the participle סֹפְדִים (sofedim, “mourning”) seems odd modifying “breasts.” The translation above assumes a twofold emendation: (1) שָׁדַיִם (shadayim, “breasts”) is emended to [ם]שָׂדַי (saday[m], “field,” a term that also appears in Isa 56:9). The final mem (ם) would be enclitic in this case, not a plural indicator. (The Hebrew noun שָׂדֶה (sadeh, “field”) forms its plural with an וֹת- [-ot] ending). (2) The plural participle סֹפְדִים is emended to סְפֹדָה (sÿfodah), a lengthened imperatival form, meaning “mourn.” For an overview of various suggestions that have been made for this difficult line, see Oswalt, 586, n. 12).

13.anchor: in the city filled with revelry.
13.note: sn This same phrase is used in 22:2.

14.note: tn Heb “the joy of wild donkeys, a pasture for flocks.”

15.anchor: and the orchard will be considered a forest.
15.note: sn The same statement appears in 29:17b, where, in conjunction with the preceding line, it appears to picture a reversal. Here it seems to depict supernatural growth. The desert will blossom into an orchard, and the trees of the orchard will multiply and grow tall, becoming a forest.

16.anchor: and fairness will live in the orchard.
16.note: sn This new era of divine blessing will also include a moral/ethical transformation, as justice and fairness fill the land and replace the social injustice so prevalent in Isaiah’s time.

17.anchor: and result in lasting security.
17.note: tn Heb “and the work of fairness [will be] calmness and security forever.”

18.anchor: and in safe, quiet places.
18.note: tn Or “in safe resting places”; NAB, NRSV “quiet resting places.”

19.anchor: and the city is annihilated,
19.note: tn Heb “and in humiliation the city is laid low.”

20.anchor: you who let your ox and donkey graze.
20.note: tn Heb “who set free the foot of the ox and donkey”; NIV “letting your cattle and donkeys range free.”sn This verse seems to anticipate a time when fertile land is available to cultivate and crops are so abundant that the farm animals can be allowed to graze freely.

1.note: tn Heb “consequently adding sin to sin.”

2.anchor: and looking for safety in Egypt’s protective shade.
2.note: tn Heb “to seek protection in the protection of Pharaoh, and to seek refuge in the shade of Egypt.”

4.anchor: and his messengers arrive at Hanes,
4.note: sn Zoan was located in the Egyptian delta in the north; Hanes was located somewhere in southern region of lower Egypt, south of Memphis; the exact location is debated.

5.anchor: all will be put to shame
5.note: tn The present translation follows the marginal (Qere) reading of the Hebrew text; the consonantal text (Kethib) has “made to stink, decay.”

6.anchor: to a nation that cannot help them.
6.note: sn This verse describes messengers from Judah transporting wealth to Egypt in order to buy Pharaoh’s protection through a treaty.

7.anchor: ‘Proud one
7.note: tn Heb “Rahab” (רַהַב, rahav), which also appears as a name for Egypt in Ps 87:4. The epithet is also used in the OT for a mythical sea monster symbolic of chaos. See the note at 51:9. A number of English versions use the name “Rahab” (e.g., ASV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV) while others attempt some sort of translation (cf. CEV “a helpless monster”; TEV, NLT “the Harmless Dragon”).

7.anchor: who is silenced.’”
7.note: tn The MT reads “Rahab, they, sitting.” The translation above assumes an emendation of הֵם שָׁבֶת (hem shavet) to הַמָּשְׁבָּת (hammashbat), a Hophal participle with prefixed definite article, meaning “the one who is made to cease,” i.e., “destroyed,” or “silenced.” See HALOT 444-45 s.v. ישׁב.

8.note: sn Recording the message will enable the prophet to use it in the future as evidence that God warned his people of impending judgment and clearly spelled out the nation’s guilt. An official record of the message will also serve as proof of the prophet’s authority as God’s spokesman.

9.note: tn Or perhaps, “instruction” (so NASB, NIV, NRSV); NCV, TEV “teachings.”

10.anchor: relate deceptive messages.
10.note: tn Heb “Tell us smooth things, see deceptive things.”

11.anchor: Remove from our presence the Holy One of Israel.”
11.note: sn See the note on the phrase “the Holy One of Israel” in 1:4.

12.anchor: and rely on that kind of behavior.
12.note: tn Heb “and you lean on it”; NAB “and depend on it.”

13.anchor: it crumbles suddenly, in a flash.
13.note: tn The verse reads literally, “So this sin will become for you like a breach ready to fall, bulging on a high wall, the breaking of which comes suddenly, in a flash.” Their sin produces guilt and will result in judgment. Like a wall that collapses their fall will be swift and sudden.

14.anchor: or to skim off water from a cistern.”
14.note: tn On the meaning of גֶבֶא (geveh, “cistern”) see HALOT 170 s.v.

15.anchor: if you calmly trusted in me you would find strength,
15.note: tn Heb “in quietness and in trust is your strength” (NASB and NRSV both similar).

17.anchor: until the remaining few are as isolated
17.note: tn Heb “until you are left” (so NAB, NASB, NRSV).

18.anchor: all who wait for him in faith will be blessed.
18.note: tn Heb “Blessed are all who wait for him.”

19.anchor: when he hears it, he will respond to you.
19.note: tn Heb “he will indeed show you mercy at the sound of your crying out; when he hears, he will answer you.”

20.anchor: your eyes will see them.
20.note: tn Heb “but your teachers will no longer be hidden, your eyes will be seeing your teachers.” The translation assumes that the form מוֹרֶיךָ (morekha) is a plural participle, referring to spiritual leaders such as prophets and priests. Another possibility is that the form is actually singular (see GKC 273-74 §93.ss) or a plural of respect, referring to God as the master teacher. See HALOT 560-61 s.v. III מוֹרֶה. For discussion of the views, see J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:560.

21.anchor: “This is the correct
21.note: tn The word “correct’ is supplied in the translation for clarification.

22.anchor: and your gold-plated images.
22.note: tn Heb “the covering of your gold image.”

23.anchor: At that time
23.note: tn Or “in that day” (KJV).

24.anchor: will eat seasoned feed winnowed with a shovel and pitchfork.
24.note: sn Crops will be so abundant that even the work animals will eat well.

25.anchor: at the time of
25.note: tn Or “in the day of” (KJV).

26.note: tn Heb “the injury of his wound.” The joining of synonyms emphasizes the severity of the wound. Another option is to translate, “the wound of his blow.” In this case the pronominal suffix might refer to the Lord, not the people, yielding the translation, “the wound which he inflicted.”

27.anchor: and his word is like destructive fire.
27.note: tn Heb “his lips are full of anger, and his tongue is like consuming fire.” The Lord’s lips and tongue are used metonymically for his word (or perhaps his battle cry; see v. 31).

28.anchor: he puts a bit into the mouth of the nations and leads them to destruction.
28.note: tn Heb “and a bit that leads astray [is] in the jaws of the peoples.” Here the nations are likened to horse that can be controlled by a bit placed in its mouth. In this case the Lord uses his sovereign control over the “horse” to lead it to its demise.

29.note: tn Heb “[you will have] joy of heart, like the one going with a flute to enter the mountain of the Lord to the Rock of Israel.” The image here is not a foundational rock, but a rocky cliff where people could hide for protection (for example, the fortress of Masada).

30.anchor: with furious anger and flaming, destructive fire,
30.note: tn Heb “and a flame of consuming fire.”

31.note: tn Heb “Indeed by the voice of the Lord Assyria will be shattered.”

32.anchor: and he will attack them with his weapons.
32.note: tn The Hebrew text reads literally, “and with battles of brandishing [weapons?] he will fight against him.” Some prefer to emend וּבְמִלְחֲמוֹת (uvÿmilkhamot, “and with battles of”) to וּבִמְחֹלוֹת (uvimkholot, “and with dancing”). Note the immediately preceding references to musical instruments.

33.anchor: The firewood is piled high on it.
33.note: tn Heb “its pile of wood, fire and wood one makes abundant.”sn Apparently this alludes to some type of funeral rite.

1.anchor: the world and everything that lives in it.
1.note: tn Heb “the world and its offspring”; NASB “the world and all that springs from it.”

3.anchor: the hills will soak up their blood.
3.note: tn Heb “hills will dissolve from their blood.”

4.anchor: or a fig withers and falls from a tree.
4.note: tn Heb “like the withering of a leaf from a vine, and like the withering from a fig tree.”

5.anchor: Look, it now descends on Edom,
5.note: sn Edom is mentioned here as epitomizing the hostile nations that oppose God.

6.note: tn Heb “great” (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV).

7.anchor: as well as strong bulls.
7.note: tn Heb “and bulls along with strong ones.” Perhaps this refers to the leaders.

8.anchor: a time when he will repay Edom for her hostility toward Zion.
8.note: tn Heb “a year of repayment for the strife of Zion.” The translation assumes that רִיב (riv) refers to Edom’s hostility toward Zion. Another option is to understand רִיב (riv) as referring to the Lord’s taking up Zion’s cause. In this case one might translate, “a time when he will repay Edom and vindicate Zion.”

9.note: tn Heb “her”; the referent (Edom) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

10.anchor: Night and day it will burn;
10.note: tn Heb “it will not be extinguished.”

11.anchor: and the plumb line
11.note: tn Heb “stones,” i.e., the stones used in a plumb bob.

11.anchor: of destruction.
11.note: sn The metaphor in v. 11b emphasizes that God has carefully planned Edom’s demise.

12.anchor: and all her officials will disappear.
12.note: tn Heb “will be nothing”; NCV, TEV, NLT “will all be gone.”

13.anchor: ostriches will live there.
13.note: tc Heb “and she will be a settlement for wild dogs, a dwelling place for ostriches.” The translation assumes an emendation of חָצִיר (khatsir, “grass”) to חָצֵר (khatser, “settlement”). One of the Qumran scrolls of Isaiah (1QIsaa) supports this emendation (cf. HALOT 344 s.v. II חָצִיר)

14.anchor: and make for themselves a nest.
14.note: tn Heb “and will find for themselves a resting place.”

15.anchor: Yes, hawks
15.note: tn The precise meaning of דַּיָּה (dayyah) is uncertain, though the term appears to refer to some type of bird of prey, perhaps a vulture.

16.anchor: and his own spirit gathers them.
16.note: tn Heb “and his spirit, he gathers them.” The pronominal suffix (“them”) is feminine plural, referring to the birds mentioned in v. 15b or to all of the creatures listed in vv. 14b-15 (all of which are identified with feminine nouns).

17.anchor: They will live there
17.note: tn Heb “will possess it” (so NIV); NCV “they will own that land forever.”

1.note: tn Heb “his”; the referent (the Lord) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

1.anchor: deliverance, like walls and a rampart, makes it secure.
1.note: tn Heb “deliverance he makes walls and a rampart.”

3.anchor: for they trust in you.
3.note: tn Heb “[one of] firm purpose you will keep [in] peace, peace, for in you he possesses trust.” The Hebrew term יֵצֶר (yetser) refers to what one devises in the mind; סָמוּךְ (samukh) probably functions here like an attributive adjective and carries the nuance “firm.” So the phrase literally means, “a firm purpose,” but as the object of the verb “keep, guard,” it must stand by metonymy for the one(s) who possess a firm purpose. In this context the “righteous nation” (v. 2) is probably in view and the “firm purpose” refers to their unwavering faith in God’s vindication (see 25:9). In this context שָׁלוֹם (shalom, “peace”), which is repeated for emphasis, likely refers to national security, not emotional or psychological composure (see vv. 1-2). The passive participle בָּטוּחַ (batuakh) expresses a state that results from the subject’s action.

4.anchor: , an enduring protector!
4.note: tc The Hebrew text has “for in Yah, the Lord, an everlasting rock.” Some have suggested that the phrase בְּיָהּ (beyah, “in Yah”) is the result of dittography. A scribe seeing כִּי יְהוָה (ki yÿhvah) in his original text would somehow have confused the letters and accidentally inserted בְּיָהּ between the words (bet and kaf [ב and כ] can be confused in later script phases). A number of English versions retain both divine names for emphasis (ESV, NIV, NKJV, NRSV, NLT). One of the Qumran texts (1QIsaa) confirms the MT reading as well.

5.anchor: he brings it down to the ground,
5.note: tn The translation assumes that יַשְׁפִּילֶנָּה (yashpilennah) goes with the preceding words “an elevated town,” and that יַשְׁפִּילָהּ (yashpilah) belongs with the following words, “to the ground.” See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:469, n. 7.

7.anchor: the path of the righteous that you make is straight.
7.note: tc The Hebrew text has, “upright, the path of the righteous you make level.” There are three possible ways to translate this line. Some take יָשָׁר (yashar) as a divine title: “O Upright One” (cf. NASB, NIV, NKJV, NRSV, NLT). Others regard יָשָׁר as the result of dittography (מֵישָׁרִים יָשָׁר ַמעְגַּל, mesharim yashar ma’gal) and do not include it in the translation. Another possibility is to keep יָשָׁר and render the line as “the path of the righteous that you prepare is straight.” sn The metaphor of a level/smooth road/path may refer to their morally upright manner of life (see v. 8a), but verse 7b, which attributes the smooth path to the Lord, suggests that the Lord’s vindication and blessing may be the reality behind the metaphor here.

8.anchor: We desire your fame and reputation to grow.
8.note: tn Heb “your name and your remembrance [is] the desire of [our?] being.”

9.anchor: those who live in the world learn about justice.
9.note: tn The translation understands צֶדֶק (tsedeq) in the sense of “justice,” but it is possible that it carries the nuance “righteousness,” in which case one might translate, “those who live in the world learn to live in a righteous manner” (cf. NCV).

10.anchor: Even in a land where right is rewarded, they act unjustly;
10.note: tn Heb “in a land of uprightness they act unjustly”; NRSV “they deal perversely.”

11.anchor: yes, fire will consume your enemies.
11.note: tn Heb “yes, fire, your enemies, will consume them.” Many understand the prefixed verb form to be jussive and translate, “let [fire] consume” (cf. NAB, NIV, NRSV). The mem suffixed to the verb may be enclitic; if a pronominal suffix, it refers back to “your enemies.”

12.anchor: for even all we have accomplished, you have done for us.
12.note: tc Some suggest emending גַּם כָּל (gam kol, “even all”) to כִּגְמֻל (kigmul, “according to the deed[s] of”) One might then translate “for according to what our deeds deserve, you have acted on our behalf.” Nevertheless, accepting the MT as it stands, the prophet affirms that Yahweh deserved all the credit for anything Israel had accomplished.

14.note: tn The Hebrew term לָכֵן (lakhen) normally indicates a cause-effect relationship between what precedes and follows and is translated, “therefore.” Here, however, it infers the cause from the effect and brings out what is implicit in the previous statement. See BDB 487 s.v.
tn Heb “visited [for harm]” (cf. KJV, ASV); NAB, NRSV “you have punished.”

15.anchor: you have made the nation larger and revealed your splendor,
15.note: tn Or “brought honor to yourself.”

16.anchor: they uttered incantations because of your discipline.
16.note: tn The meaning of this verse is unclear. It appears to read literally, “O Lord, in distress they visit you, they pour out [?] an incantation, your discipline to them.” פָּקַד (paqad) may here carry the sense of “seek with interest” (cf. Ezek 23:21 and BDB 823 s.v.) or “seek in vain” (cf. Isa 34:16), but it is peculiar for the Lord to be the object of this verb. צָקוּן (tsaqun) may be a Qal perfect third plural form from צוּק (tsuq, “pour out, melt”), though the verb is not used of pouring out words in its two other occurrences. Because of the appearance of צַר (tsar, “distress”) in the preceding line, it is tempting to emend the form to a noun and derive it from צוּק (“be in distress”) The term לַחַשׁ (lakhash) elsewhere refers to an incantation (Isa 3:3; Jer 8:17; Eccl 10:11) or amulet (Isa 3:20). Perhaps here it refers to ritualistic prayers or to magical incantations used to ward off evil.

18.anchor: people to populate the world are not born.
18.note: tn Heb “and the inhabitants of the world do not fall.” The term נָפַל (nafal) apparently means here, “be born,” though the Qal form of the verb is not used with this nuance anywhere else in the OT. (The Hiphil appears to be used in the sense of “give birth” in v. 19, however.) The implication of verse 18b seems to be that Israel hoped its suffering would somehow end in deliverance and an increase in population. The phrase “inhabitants of the world” seems to refer to the human race in general, but the next verse, which focuses on Israel’s dead, suggests the referent may be more limited.

19.anchor: and the earth will bring forth its dead spirits.
19.note: sn It is not certain whether the resurrection envisioned here is intended to be literal or figurative. A comparison with 25:8 and Dan 12:2 suggests a literal interpretation, but Ezek 37:1-14 uses resurrection as a metaphor for deliverance from exile and the restoration of the nation (see Isa 27:12-13).

20.anchor: until his angry judgment is over!
20.note: tn Heb “until anger passes by.”

21.anchor: it will no longer cover up its slain.
21.note: sn This implies that rampant bloodshed is one of the reasons for divine judgment. See the note at 24:5.

1.note: tn Heb “Add year to year, let your festivals occur in cycles.” This is probably a sarcastic exhortation to the people to keep up their religious rituals, which will not prevent the coming judgment. See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:527.

2.anchor: and become like an altar hearth
2.note: tn The term אֲרִיאֵל (’ari’el, “Ariel”) is the word translated “altar hearth” here. The point of the simile is not entirely clear. Perhaps the image likens Jerusalem’s coming crisis to a sacrificial fire.

3.anchor: I will besiege you with troops;
3.note: tn The meaning of מֻצָּב (mutsav) is not certain. Because of the parallelism (note “siege works”), some translate “towers.” The noun is derived from נָצַב (natsav, “take one’s stand”) and may refer to the troops stationed outside the city to prevent entrance or departure.

4.anchor: from the dust you will chirp as if muttering an incantation.
4.note: tn Heb “and from the dust your word will chirp.” The words “as if muttering an incantation” are supplied in the translation for clarification. See the parallelism and 8:19.

5.anchor: the horde of tyrants
5.note: tn Or “violent men”; cf. NASB “the ruthless ones.”

6.note: tn Heb “from the Lord who commands armies [traditionally, the Lord of hosts] there will be visitation.” The third feminine singular passive verb form תִּפָּקֵד (tippaqed, “she/it will be visited”) is used here in an impersonal sense. See GKC 459 §144.b.

8.anchor: only to awaken and find that he is still weak and his thirst unquenched.
8.note: tn Or “that he is faint and that he [or “his appetite”] longs [for water].”

9.note: tc Some prefer to emend the perfect form of the verb to an imperative (e.g., NAB, NCV, NRSV), since the people are addressed in the immediately preceding and following contexts.

10.anchor: a strong urge to sleep deeply.
10.note: tn Heb “a disposition [or “spirit”] of deep sleep.” Through this mixed metaphor (sleep is likened to a liquid which one pours and in turn symbolizes spiritual dullness) the prophet emphasizes that God himself has given the people over to their spiritual insensitivity as a form of judgment.

11.anchor: To you this entire prophetic revelation
11.note: tn Heb “vision” (so NASB, NIV, NRSV).

11.anchor: is like words in a sealed scroll. When they hand it to one who can read
11.note: tn Heb “one who knows a/the scroll.”

12.anchor: Or when they hand the scroll to one who can’t read
12.note: tn Heb “and if the scroll is handed to one who does not know a scroll.”

12.anchor: and say, “Read this,” he says, “I can’t read.”
12.note: tn Heb “I do not know a scroll.”

13.anchor: nothing but man-made ritual.
13.note: tn Heb “their fear of me is a commandment of men that has been taught.”

14.anchor: the sages will have no explanations.”
14.note: tn Heb “the wisdom of their wise ones will perish, the discernment of their discerning ones will keep hidden.”

15.anchor: “Who sees us? Who knows what we’re doing?”
15.note: tn The rhetorical questions suggest the answer, “no one.” They are confident that their deeds are hidden from others, including God.

16.anchor: Should the thing made say
16.note: tn Heb “that the thing made should say.”

17.anchor: and the orchard will be considered a forest.
17.note: sn The meaning of this verse is debated, but it seems to depict a reversal in fortunes. The mighty forest of Lebanon (symbolic of the proud and powerful, see 2:13; 10:34) will be changed into a common orchard, while the common orchard (symbolic of the oppressed and lowly) will grow into a great forest. See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:538.

18.anchor: and the eyes of the blind will be able to see through deep darkness.
18.note: tn Heb “and out of gloom and darkness the eyes of the blind will see.”sn Perhaps this depicts the spiritual transformation of the once spiritually insensitive nation (see vv. 10-12, cf. also 6:9-10).

19.anchor: the poor among humankind will take delight
19.note: tn Or “will rejoice” (NIV, NCV, NLT).

19.anchor: in the Holy One of Israel.
19.note: sn See the note on the phrase “the Holy One of Israel” in 1:4.

20.anchor: and all those who love to do wrong will be eliminated
20.note: tn Heb “and all the watchers of wrong will be cut off.”

21.anchor: and deprive the innocent of justice by making false charges.
21.note: tn Heb “and deprive by emptiness the innocent.”

22.anchor: their faces will no longer show their embarrassment.
22.note: tn Heb “and his face will no longer be pale.”

23.anchor: they will respect
23.note: tn Or “fear,” in the sense of “stand in awe of.”

24.anchor: those who complain will acquire insight.
24.note: tn Heb “will learn instruction”; cf. NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT “will accept instruction.”

1.note: tn Or “Arabah” (NASB); NAB, NIV, TEV “desert.”

2.note: tn Or “glory” (KJV, NIV, NRSV); also a second time later in this verse.

3.anchor: steady the knees that shake!
3.note: tn Heb “staggering knees”; KJV, ASV, NRSV “feeble knees”; NIV “knees that give way.”

4.anchor: With divine retribution he comes to deliver you.”
4.note: tn The jussive form וְיֹשַׁעֲכֶם (vÿyosha’akhem), which is subordinated to the preceding imperfect with vav conjunctive, indicates purpose.

6.note: tn Or “Arabah” (NASB); KJV, NIV, NRSV, NLT “desert.”

8.note: tn In this context “fools” are those who are morally corrupt, not those with limited intellectual capacity.

9.anchor: no ferocious wild animals will be on it
9.note: tn Heb “will go up on it”; TEV “will pass that way.”

10.anchor: grief and suffering will disappear.
10.note: tn Heb “grief and groaning will flee”; KJV “sorrow and sighing shall flee away.”

1.anchor: In the fourteenth year of King Hezekiah’s reign,
1.note: tn The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on “in the future” in 2:2.

2.anchor: The king of Assyria sent his chief adviser
2.note: sn For a discussion of this title see M. Cogan and H. Tadmor, II Kings (AB), 229-30.

2.anchor: from Lachish to King Hezekiah in Jerusalem,
2.note: map For location see Map5-B1; Map6-F3; Map7-E2; Map8-F2; Map10-B3; JP1-F4; JP2-F4; JP3-F4; JP4-F4.

2.anchor: along with a large army. The chief adviser
2.note: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the chief adviser) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

2.anchor: stood at the conduit of the upper pool which is located on the road to the field where they wash and dry cloth.
2.note: tn Heb “the field of the washer”; traditionally “the fuller’s field” (so KJV, ASV, NAB, NASB, NRSV).

4.anchor: The chief adviser said to them, “Tell Hezekiah: ‘This is what the great king, the king of Assyria, says: “What is your source of confidence?
4.note: tn Heb “What is this object of trust in which you are trusting?”

5.anchor: Your claim to have a strategy and military strength is just empty talk.
5.note: tn Heb “you say only a word of lips, counsel and might for battle.” Sennacherib’s message appears to be in broken Hebrew at this point. The phrase “word of lips” refers to mere or empty talk in Prov 14:23.

9.anchor: Certainly you will not refuse one of my master’s minor officials and trust in Egypt for chariots and horsemen.
9.note: tn Heb “How can you turn back the face of an official [from among] the least of my master’s servants and trust in Egypt for chariots and horsemen?” In vv. 8-9 the chief adviser develops further the argument begun in v. 6. His reasoning seems to be as follows: “In your weakened condition you obviously need military strength. Agree to the king’s terms and I will personally give you more horses than you are capable of outfitting. If I, a mere minor official, am capable of giving you such military might, just think what power the king has. There is no way the Egyptians can match our strength. It makes much better sense to deal with us.”

10.anchor: told me, ‘March up against this land and destroy it!’”’”
10.note: sn In v. 10 the chief adviser develops further the argument begun in v. 7. He claims that Hezekiah has offended the Lord and that the Lord has commissioned Assyria as his instrument of discipline and judgment.

11.anchor: Eliakim, Shebna, and Joah said to the chief adviser, “Speak to your servants in Aramaic,
11.note: sn Aramaic was the diplomatic language of the Assyrian empire.

11.anchor: for we understand it. Don’t speak with us in the Judahite dialect
11.note: tn Or “in Hebrew” (NIV, NCV, NLT); NAB, NASB “in Judean.”

12.anchor: But the chief adviser said, “My master did not send me to speak these words only to your master and to you.
12.note: tn Heb “To your master and to you did my master send me to speak these words?” The rhetorical question expects a negative answer.

12.anchor: His message is also for the men who sit on the wall, for they will eat their own excrement and drink their own urine along with you!”
12.note: tn Heb “[Is it] not [also] to the men…?” The rhetorical question expects the answer, “Yes, it is.”sn The chief adviser alludes to the horrible reality of siege warfare, when the starving people in the besieged city would resort to eating and drinking anything to stay alive.

13.anchor: The chief adviser then stood there and called out loudly in the Judahite dialect,
13.note: tn The Hebrew text includes “and he said.”

16.anchor: Don’t listen to Hezekiah!’ For this is what the king of Assyria says, ‘Send me a token of your submission and surrender to me.
16.note: tn Heb “make with me a blessing and come out to me.”

18.anchor: will rescue us.” Has any of the gods of the nations rescued his land from the power of the king of Assyria?
18.note: tn Heb “Have the gods of the nations rescued, each his land, from the hand of the king of Assyria?” The rhetorical question expects the answer, “Of course not!”

19.anchor: Where are the gods of Hamath and Arpad? Where are the gods of Sepharvaim?
19.note: tn The rhetorical questions in v. 34a suggest the answer, “Nowhere, they seem to have disappeared in the face of Assyria’s might.”

19.anchor: Indeed, did any gods rescue Samaria
19.note: map For location see Map2-B1; Map4-D3; Map5-E2; Map6-A4; Map7-C1.

19.anchor: from my power?
19.note: tn Heb “that they rescued Samaria from my hand?” But this gives the impression that the gods of Sepharvaim were responsible for protecting Samaria, which is obviously not the case. The implied subject of the plural verb “rescued” must be the generic “gods of the nations/lands” (vv. 18, 20).

20.anchor: rescue Jerusalem from my power?’”
20.note: tn Heb “that the Lord might rescue Jerusalem from my hand?” The logic runs as follows: Since no god has ever been able to withstand the Assyrian onslaught, how can the people of Jerusalem possibly think the Lord will rescue them?

22.anchor: Eliakim son of Hilkiah, the palace supervisor, accompanied by Shebna the scribe and Joah son of Asaph, the secretary, went to Hezekiah with their clothes torn in grief
22.note: tn Heb “with their clothes torn”; the words “in grief” have been supplied in the translation to indicate that this was done as a sign of grief and mourning.

1.anchor: But they do not rely on the Holy One of Israel
1.note: sn See the note on the phrase “the Holy One of Israel” in 1:4.

2.note: sn That is, Egypt.
tn Heb “and against the help of the doers of sin.”

3.anchor: Together they will perish.
3.note: tn Heb “together all of them will come to an end.”

4.anchor: to do battle on Mount Zion and on its hill.
4.note: tn Some prefer to translate the phrase לִצְבֹּא עַל (litsbo’ ’al) as “fight against,” but the following context pictures the Lord defending, not attacking, Zion.

5.anchor: as he passes over
5.note: tn The only other occurrence of this verb is in Exod 12:13, 23, 27, where the Lord “passes over” (i.e., “spares”) the Israelite households as he comes to judge their Egyptian oppressors. The noun פֶּסַח (pesakh, “Passover”) is derived from the verb. The use of the verb in Isa 31:5 is probably an intentional echo of the Exodus event. As in the days of Moses the Lord will spare his people as he comes to judge their enemies.

6.anchor: You Israelites! Return to the one against whom you have so blatantly rebelled!
6.note: tn Heb “Return to the one [against] whom the sons of Israel made deep rebellion.” The syntax is awkward here. A preposition is omitted by ellipsis after the verb (see GKC 446 §138.f, n. 2), and there is a shift from direct address (note the second plural imperative “return”) to the third person (note “they made deep”). For other examples of abrupt shifts in person in poetic style, see GKC 462 §144.p.

7.anchor: For at that time
7.note: tn Or “in that day” (KJV).

7.anchor: everyone will get rid of
7.note: tn Heb “reject” (so NIV); NRSV, TEV, CEV, NLT “throw away.”

7.anchor: the silver and gold idols your hands sinfully made.
7.note: tn Heb “the idols of their idols of silver and their idols of gold which your hands made for yourselves [in] sin.” חָטָא (khata’, “sin”) is understood as an adverbial accusative of manner. See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:573, n. 4.

8.anchor: They will run away from this sword
8.note: tn Heb “he will flee for himself from before a sword.”

9.note: sn The “fire” and “firepot” here symbolize divine judgment, which is heating up like a fire in Jerusalem, waiting to be used against the Assyrians when they attack the city.

2.note: tn Heb “was happy with”; NAB, NASB “was pleased”; NIV “received the envoys gladly.”

2.anchor: them and showed them his storehouse with its silver, gold, spices, and high-quality olive oil, as well as his whole armory and everything in his treasuries. Hezekiah showed them everything in his palace and in his whole kingdom.
2.note: tn Heb “there was nothing which Hezekiah did not show them in his house and in all his kingdom.”

4.anchor: Isaiah
4.note: tn Heb “he”; the referent (Isaiah) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

6.anchor: ‘Look, a time is coming when everything in your palace and the things your ancestors
6.note: tn Heb “fathers” (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV).

7.anchor: ‘Some of your very own descendants whom you father
7.note: tn Heb “Some of your sons, who go out from you, whom you father.”

8.anchor: ’s word which you have announced is appropriate.”
8.note: tn Heb “good” (so KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT); NAB “favorable.”
tn Heb “and he said.” The verb אָמַר (’amar, “say”) is sometimes used of what one thinks (that is, says to oneself).
tn Or “surely”; cf. CEV “At least.”

1.note: tn Heb “he will bring out justice” (cf. ASV, NASB, NRSV).
sn Like the ideal king portrayed in Isa 11:1-9, the servant is energized by the divine spirit and establishes justice on the earth.

2.anchor: he will not publicize himself in the streets.
2.note: tn Heb “he will not cause his voice to be heard in the street.”

3.anchor: he will faithfully make just decrees.
3.note: tn Heb “faithfully he will bring out justice” (cf. NASB, NRSV).

4.anchor: the coastlands
4.note: tn Or “islands” (NIV); NLT “distant lands beyond the sea.”

4.anchor: will wait in anticipation for his decrees.”
4.note: tn Or “his law” (KJV, ASV, NASB, NIV) or “his instruction” (NLT).

5.anchor: and life to those who live on it:
5.note: tn Heb “and spirit [i.e., “breath”] to the ones walking in it” (NAB, NASB, and NRSV all similar).

6.note: sn Light here symbolizes deliverance from bondage and oppression; note the parallelism in 49:6b and in 51:4-6.
tn Or “the Gentiles” (so KJV, ASV, NIV); the same Hebrew word can be translated “nations” or “Gentiles” depending on the context.

7.anchor: to release prisoners
7.note: sn This does not refer to hardened, dangerous criminals, who would have been executed for their crimes in ancient Near Eastern society. This verse refers to political prisoners or victims of social injustice.

9.note: tn Heb “before they sprout up, I cause you to hear.” The pronoun “you” is plural, referring to the people of Israel. In this verse “the former things” are the Lord’s earlier predictive oracles which have come to pass, while “the new things” are predicted events that have not yet begun to take place. “The former things” are earlier events in Israel’s history which God announced beforehand, such as the Exodus (see 43:16-18). “The new things” are the predictions about the servant (42:1-7). and may also include Cyrus’ conquests (41:25-27).

10.anchor: you coastlands
10.note: tn Or “islands” (NASB, NIV); NLT “distant coastlands.”

12.anchor: let them praise his deeds in the coastlands.
12.note: tn Heb “and his praise in the coastlands [or “islands”] let them declare.”

13.anchor: he shows his enemies his power.
13.note: tn Or perhaps, “he triumphs over his enemies” (cf. NIV); NLT “will crush all his enemies.”

14.note: sn The imagery depicts the Lord as a warrior who is eager to fight and can no longer hold himself back from the attack.

15.anchor: and dry up pools of water.
15.note: sn The imagery of this verse, which depicts the Lord bringing a curse of infertility to the earth, metaphorically describes how the Lord will destroy his enemies.

16.anchor: and level out the rough ground.
16.note: tn Heb “and the rough ground into a level place.”

17.anchor: will turn back and be utterly humiliated,
17.note: tn Heb “be ashamed with shame”; ASV, NASB “be utterly put to shame.”

18.anchor: Take notice,
18.note: tn Heb “look to see”; NAB, NCV “look and see”; NRSV “look up and see.”

19.anchor: My covenant partner,
19.note: tc The precise meaning of מְשֻׁלָּם (mÿshullam) in this context is uncertain. In later biblical Hebrew the form (which appears to be a Pual participle from the root שָׁלַם, shalam) occurs as a proper name, Meshullam. The Pual of שָׁלַם (“be complete”) is attested with the meaning “repaid, requited,” but that makes little sense here. BDB 1023 s.v. שָׁלַם relates the form to the denominative verb שָׁלַם (“be at peace”) and paraphrases “one in a covenant of peace” (J. N. Oswalt suggests “the covenanted one”; Isaiah [NICOT], 2:128, n. 59) Some emend the form to מֹשְׁלָם (moshÿlam, “their ruler”) or to מְשֻׁלָּחִי (mÿshullakhi, “my sent [or “commissioned”] one”), which fits nicely in the parallelism (note “my messenger” in the previous line). The translation above assumes an emendation to כְּמוֹ שֹׁלְמִי (kÿmo sholÿmi, “like my ally”). Isaiah uses כְּמוֹ in 30:22 and perhaps 51:5; for שֹׁלְמי (“my ally”) see Ps 7:5 HT (7:4 ET).

19.anchor: , is truly blind.
19.note: tn Heb “Who is blind but my servant, and deaf like my messenger I send? Who is blind like my commissioned one, blind like the servant of the Lord?” The point of the rhetorical questions is that no one is as blind/deaf as this servant. In this context the Lord’s “servant” is exiled Israel (cf. 41:8-9), which is spiritually blind and deaf and has failed to fulfill God’s purpose for it. This servant stands in contrast to the ideal “Israel” of the servant songs.

20.anchor: You see
20.note: tn The consonantal text (Kethib) has a perfect, 2nd person masculine singular; the marginal reading (Qere) has an infinitive absolute, which functions here as a finite verb.

20.anchor: many things, but don’t comprehend;
20.note: tn Heb “but you do not guard [i.e., retain in your memory]”; NIV “but have paid no attention.”

21.anchor: by magnifying his law and displaying it.
21.note: tn Heb “The Lord was pleased for the sake of his righteousness [or “justice”], he was magnifying [the] law and was making [it] glorious.” The Lord contrasts his good intentions for the people with their present crisis (v. 22). To demonstrate his just character and attract the nations, the Lord wanted to showcase his law among and through Israel (Deut 4:5-8). But Israel disobeyed (v. 24) and failed to carry out their commission.

22.anchor: they were carried away as plunder, and no one says, “Bring that back!”
22.note: tn Heb “they became loot and there was no one rescuing, plunder and there was no one saying, ‘Bring back’.”

23.anchor: Who will listen attentively in the future?
23.note: tn The interrogative particle is understood in the second line by ellipsis (note the preceding line).

24.anchor: they disobeyed his law.
24.note: tn Heb “they were not willing in his ways to walk, and they did not listen to his law.”

25.anchor: it burned against them, but they did notice.
25.note: tn Heb “and it burned against him, but he did not set [it] upon [the] heart.”

1.note: tn The pronominal suffix is second masculine plural. The identity of the addressee is uncertain: (1) God’s people may be addressed, or (2) the unidentified heralds commanded to comfort Jerusalem.

2.note: tn Heb “for she has received from the hand of the Lord double.” The principle of the double portion in punishment is also seen in Jer 16:18; 17:18 and Rev 18:6. For examples of the double portion in Israelite law, see Exod 22:4, 7, 9 (double restitution by a thief) and Deut 21:17 (double inheritance portion for the firstborn).

5.anchor: For
5.note: tn Or “indeed.”

5.anchor: has decreed it.”
5.note: tn Heb “the mouth of the Lord has spoken” (so NASB, NIV, NRSV).

6.note: tn Heb “and all his loyalty.” The antecedent of the third masculine suffix is בָּשָׂר (basar, “flesh”), which refers collectively to mankind. The LXX, apparently understanding the antecedent as “grass,” reads “glory,” but חֶסֶד (khesed) rarely, if ever, has this nuance. The normal meaning of חֶסֶד (“faithfulness, loyalty, devotion”) fits very well in the argument. Human beings and their faithfulness (verbal expressions of faithfulness are specifically in view; cf. NRSV “constancy”) are short-lived and unreliable, in stark contrast to the decrees and promises of the eternal God.

7.anchor: Surely humanity
7.note: tn Heb “the people” (so KJV, ASV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV).

8.anchor: but the decree of our God is forever reliable.”
8.note: tn Heb “but the word of our God stands forever.” In this context the divine “word” specifically refers to his decreed promise assuring Jerusalem that her suffering is over and his glorious return imminent (vv. 1-5).

9.anchor: Shout out loudly, O herald Jerusalem!
9.note: tn The second feminine singular imperatives are addressed to personified Zion/Jerusalem, who is here told to ascend a high hill and proclaim the good news of the Lord’s return to the other towns of Judah. Isa 41:27 and 52:7 speak of a herald sent to Zion, but the masculine singular form מְבַשֵּׂר (mÿvaser) is used in these verses, in contrast to the feminine singular form מְבַשֶּׂרֶת (mÿvaseret) employed in 40:9, where Zion is addressed as a herald.

10.anchor: his prize goes before him.
10.note: tn As the Lord returns to Jerusalem as a victorious warrior, he brings with him the spoils of victory, called here his “reward” and “prize.” These terms might also be translated “wages” and “recompense.” Verse 11 indicates that his rescued people, likened to a flock of sheep, are his reward.

11.anchor: he carries them close to his heart;
11.note: tn Heb “in his bosom” (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NRSV), an expression which reflects closeness and protective care.

12.anchor: or the hills on scales?
12.note: sn The implied answer to the rhetorical questions of v. 12 is “no one but the Lord. The Lord, and no other, created the world. Like a merchant weighing out silver or commodities on a scale, the Lord established the various components of the physical universe in precise proportions.

13.anchor: or gives him instruction as his counselor?
13.note: tn Heb “or [as] the man of his counsel causes him to know?”

14.anchor: or instructs him in skillful design?
14.note: tn Heb “or the way of understanding causes him to know?”sn The implied answer to the rhetorical questions in vv. 13-14 is, “No one.” In contrast to Marduk, the creator-god of Mesopotamian myths who receives help from the god of wisdom, the Lord neither needs nor receives any such advice or help. See R. Whybray, Heavenly Counsellor (SOTSMS), 64-77.

15.anchor: He lifts
15.note: tn Or “weighs” (NIV); NLT “picks up.”

15.anchor: the coastlands
15.note: tn Or “islands” (NASB, NIV, NLT).

16.anchor: its wild animals would not provide enough burnt offerings.
16.note: sn The point is that not even the Lebanon forest could supply enough wood and animals for an adequate sacrifice to the Lord.

17.anchor: they are regarded as absolutely nothing.
17.note: tn Heb “[as derived] from nothing and unformed.”

19.anchor: A craftsman casts
19.note: tn Heb “pours out”; KJV “melteth.”

20.anchor: to make
20.note: tn Or “set up” (ASV, NAB, NIV, NRSV); KJV, NASB “to prepare.”

22.anchor: and spreads it out
22.note: tn The meaning of the otherwise unattested verb מָתַח (matakh, “spread out”) is determined from the parallelism (note the corresponding verb “stretch out” in the previous line) and supported by later Hebrew and Aramaic cognates. See HALOT 654 s.v. *מתה.

22.anchor: like a pitched tent.
22.note: tn Heb “like a tent [in which] to live”; NAB, NASB “like a tent to dwell (live NIV, NRSV) in.”

25.anchor: says the Holy One.
25.note: sn See the note on the phrase “the Holy One of Israel” in 1:4.

26.anchor: He is the one who leads out their ranks;
26.note: tn Heb “the one who brings out by number their host.” The stars are here likened to a huge army that the Lord leads out. Perhaps the next line pictures God calling roll. If so, the final line may be indicating that none of them dares “go AWOL.” (“AWOL” is a military acronym for “absent without leave.”)

27.anchor: My God is not concerned with my vindication”?
27.note: tn Heb “and from my God my justice passes away”; NRSV “my right is disregarded by my God.”

28.anchor: there is no limit to his wisdom.
28.note: sn Exiled Israel’s complaint (v. 27) implies that God might be limited in some way. Perhaps he, like so many of the pagan gods, has died. Or perhaps his jurisdiction is limited to Judah and does not include Babylon. Maybe he is unable to devise an adequate plan to rescue his people, or is unable to execute it. But v. 28 affirms that he is not limited temporally or spatially nor is his power and wisdom restricted in any way. He can and will deliver his people, if they respond in hopeful faith (v. 31a).

30.anchor: even strong young men clumsily stumble.
30.note: tn Heb “stumbling they stumble.” The verbal idea is emphasized by the infinitive absolute.

31.anchor: they rise up as if they had eagles’ wings,
31.note: tn Heb “they rise up [on] wings like eagles” (TEV similar).

1.note: tc The Hebrew text has יִפְרֶה (yifreh, “will bear fruit,” from פָּרָה, parah), but the ancient versions, as well as the parallelism suggest that יִפְרַח (yifrakh, “will sprout”, from פָּרַח, parakh) is the better reading here. See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:276, n. 2.

2.note: tn Heb “a spirit of knowledge and fear of the Lord.” “Knowledge” is used here in its covenantal sense and refers to a recognition of God’s authority and a willingness to submit to it. See Jer 22:16. “Fear” here refers to a healthy respect for God’s authority which produces obedience. Taken together the two terms emphasize the single quality of loyalty to the Lord. This loyalty guarantees that he will make just legal decisions and implement just policies (vv. 4-5).

3.anchor: or make decisions on the basis of hearsay.
3.note: tn Heb “by what is heard by his ears”; NRSV “by what his ears hear.”

4.anchor: and order the wicked to be executed.
4.note: tn Heb “and by the breath of his lips he will kill the wicked.” The “breath of his lips” refers to his speech, specifically in this context his official decrees that the wicked oppressors be eliminated from his realm. See the preceding note.

5.anchor: integrity will be like a belt around his hips.
5.note: tn Heb “Justice will be the belt [or “undergarment”] on his waist, integrity the belt [or “undergarment”] on his hips.” The point of the metaphor is uncertain. If a belt worn outside the robe is in view, then the point might be that justice/integrity will be readily visible or that these qualities will give support to his rule. If an undergarment is in view, then the idea might be that these characteristics support his rule or that they are basic to everything else.

6.anchor: an ox and a young lion will graze together,
6.note: tc The Hebrew text reads, “and an ox, and a young lion, and a fatling together.” Since the preceding lines refer to two animals and include a verb, many emend וּמְרִיא (umÿri’, “and the fatling”) to an otherwise unattested verb יִמְרְאוּ (yimrÿ’u, “they will graze”); cf. NAB, TEV, CEV. One of the Qumran copies of Isaiah confirms this suggestion (1QIsaa). The present translation assumes this change.

7.anchor: their young will lie down together.
7.note: tn Heb “and a cow and a bear will graze – together – they will lie down, their young.” This is a case of pivot pattern; יַחְדָּו (yakhddav, “together”) goes with both the preceding and following statements.

8.note: tn Heb “one who is weaned” (cf. KJV, ASV, NASB, NRSV).

sn The transformation of the animal kingdom depicted here typifies what will occur in human society under the just rule of the ideal king (see vv. 3-5). The categories “predator-prey” (i.e., oppressor-oppressed) will no longer exist.

9.anchor: just as the waters completely cover the sea.
9.note: tn Heb “for the earth will be full of knowledge of the Lord, as the waters cover the sea.” The translation assumes that a universal kingdom is depicted here, but אֶרֶץ (’erets) could be translated “land” (see the note at v. 4). “Knowledge of the Lord” refers here to a recognition of the Lord’s sovereignty which results in a willingness to submit to his authority. See the note at v. 2.

10.anchor: At that time
10.note: tn Or “in that day” (KJV). The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on “in the future” in 2:2.

10.anchor: a root from Jesse
10.note: sn See the note at v. 1.

10.anchor: will stand like a signal flag for the nations. Nations will look to him for guidance,
10.note: tn Heb “ a root from Jesse, which stands for a signal flag of the nations, of him nations will inquire” [or “seek”].

11.anchor: At that time
11.note: tn Or “in that day” (KJV). The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on “in the future” in 2:2.

11.anchor: the sovereign master
11.note: tn The Hebrew term translated “sovereign master” here is אֲדֹנָי (’adonai).

11.anchor: will again lift his hand
11.note: tc The Hebrew text reads, “the sovereign master will again, a second time, his hand.” The auxiliary verb יוֹסִיף (yosif), which literally means “add,” needs a main verb to complete it. Consequently many emend שֵׁנִית (shenit, “a second time”) to an infinitive. Some propose the form שַׁנֹּת (shannot, a Piel infinitive construct from שָׁנָה, shanah) and relate it semantically to an Arabic cognate meaning “to be high.” If the Hebrew text is retained a verb must be supplied. “Second time” would allude back to the events of the Exodus (see vv. 15-16).

11.anchor: to reclaim
11.note: tn Or “acquire”; KJV, ASV, NASB, NRSV “recover.”

11.anchor: the remnant of his people
11.note: tn Heb “the remnant of his people who remain.”

11.anchor: from Assyria, Egypt, Pathros,
11.note: sn Perhaps a reference to Upper (i.e., southern) Egypt (so NIV, NLT; NCV “South Egypt”).

11.anchor: Cush,
11.note: tn Or “Ethiopia” (NAB, NRSV, NLT).

11.anchor: Elam, Shinar,
11.note: tn Or “Babylonia” (NIV, NCV, TEV, NLT).

11.anchor: Hamath, and the seacoasts.
11.note: tn Or perhaps, “the islands of the sea.”

12.anchor: he will gather Israel’s dispersed people
12.note: tn Or “the banished of Israel,” i.e., the exiles.

13.anchor: and Judah’s hostility
13.note: tn Heb “hostile ones of Judah.” Elsewhere when the substantival participle of צָרָר (tsarar) takes a pronominal suffix or appears in a construct relationship, the following genitive is objective. (For a list of texts see BDB 865 s.v. III צָרַר) In this case the phrase “hostile ones of Judah” means “those who are hostile toward Judah,” i.e., Judah’s enemies. However, the parallel couplet that follows suggests that Judah’s hostility toward Ephraim is in view. In this case “hostile ones of Judah” means “hostile ones from Judah.” The translation above assumes the latter, giving the immediate context priority over general usage.

14.anchor: They will take over Edom and Moab,
14.note: tn Heb “Edom and Moab [will be the place of] the outstretching of their hand,” i.e., included in their area of jurisdiction (see HALOT 648 s.v. ח(וֹ)מִשְׁלֹ).

15.anchor: he will turn it into seven dried-up streams,
15.note: tn Heb “seven streams.” The Hebrew term נַחַל (nakhal, “stream”) refers to a wadi, or seasonal stream, which runs during the rainy season, but is otherwise dry. The context (see v. 15b) here favors the translation, “dried up streams.” The number seven suggests totality and completeness. Here it indicates that God’s provision for escape will be thorough and more than capable of accommodating the returning exiles.

16.anchor: when
16.note: tn Heb “in the day” (so KJV).

1.anchor: “Don’t be afraid, for I will protect
1.note: tn Or “redeem.” See the note at 41:14. Cf. NCV “saved you”; CEV “rescued you”; NLT “ransomed you.”

2.note: tn Heb “burn” (so NASB); NAB, NRSV, NLT “consume”; NIV “set you ablaze.”

3.anchor: Ethiopia and Seba
3.note: sn Seba is not the same as Sheba in southern Arabia; cf. Gen 1:10; 1 Chr 1:9.

4.anchor: Since you are precious and special in my sight,
4.note: tn Heb “Since you are precious in my eyes and you are honored.”

7.anchor: everyone who belongs to me,
7.note: tn Heb “everyone who is called by my name” (so NASB, NIV, NRSV).

9.anchor: Who predicted earlier events for us?
9.note: tn Heb “and the former things was causing us to hear?”

10.anchor: and none will outlive me.
10.note: tn Heb “and after me, there will not be”; NASB “there will be none after Me.”

13.anchor: no one can deliver from my power;
13.note: tn Heb “hand” (so KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV); NLT “No one can oppose what I do.”

14.anchor: turning the Babylonians’ joyful shouts into mourning songs.
14.note: tn The Hebrew text reads literally, “as for the Babylonians, in ships their joyful shout.” This might be paraphrased, “even the Babylonians in the ships [over which] they joyfully shouted.” The point would be that the Lord caused the Babylonians to flee for safety in the ships in which they took such great pride. A slight change in vocalization yields the reading “into mourning songs,” which provides a good contrast with “joyful shout.” The prefixed bet (בְּ) would indicate identity.

15.anchor: , your Holy One,
15.note: sn See the note on the phrase “the Holy One of Israel” in 1:4.

17.anchor: They fell down,
17.note: tn Heb “lay down”; NAB “lie prostrate together”; CEV “lie dead”; NRSV “they lie down.”

18.anchor: “Don’t remember these earlier events;
18.note: tn Heb “the former things” (so KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV); NLT “forget all that.”

19.anchor: and paths
19.note: tn The Hebrew texts has “streams,” probably under the influence of v. 20. The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa has נתיבות (“paths”).

21.anchor: so they might praise me.”
21.note: tn Heb “[so] they might declare my praise.”

22.anchor: you did not long
22.note: tn Or “strive”; KJV, ASV, NRSV “been weary of me.”

23.anchor: I did not make you weary by demanding
23.note: tn Heb “with.” The words “by demanding” are supplied in the translation for clarification.

24.anchor: you made me weary with your evil deeds.
24.note: sn In vv. 22-24 the Lord appears to be condemning his people for failure to bring the proper sacrifices. However, this is problematic. If this refers to the nation’s behavior while in exile, such cultic service was impossible and could hardly be expected by the Lord. If this refers to the nation’s conduct before the exile, it contradicts other passages that depict Israel as bringing excessive sacrifices (see, e.g., Isa 1:11-14; Jer 6:20; Amos 4:4-5, 5:21-23). Rather than being a condemnation of Israel’s failure to bring sacrifices, these verses are better taken as a highly rhetorical comment on the worthlessness of Israel’s religious ritual. They may have brought sacrifices, but not to the Lord, for he did not accept them or even want them. See C. R. North, Second Isaiah, 127, and R. Whybray, Isaiah 40-66 (NCBC), 91.

26.anchor: You, prove to me that you are right!
26.note: tn Heb “you, tell in order that you may be right”; NAB “prove your innocence.”

27.note: tn On the meaning of the term לִיץ (lits), see HALOT 590 s.v. מֵלִיץ. This may refer to the nation’s prophets, priests, and/or kings.

28.note: tn The word “subjected” is supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.

1.anchor: let us come together for debate!
1.note: tn The Hebrew term מִשְׁפָּט (mishpat) could be translated “judgment,” but here it seems to refer to the dispute or debate between the Lord and the nations.

2.anchor: like windblown straw with his bow.
2.note: sn The point is that they are powerless before Cyrus’ military power and scatter before him.

3.anchor: he advances with great speed.
3.note: tn Heb “a way with his feet he does not come [or “enter”].” One could translate, “by a way he was not [previously] entering with his feet.” This would mean that he is advancing into new territory and expanding his conquests. The present translation assumes this is a hyperbolic description to his speedy advance. He moves so quickly he does not enter the way with his feet, i.e., his feet don’t even touch the ground. See C. R. North, Second Isaiah, 94.

4.anchor: and at the very end – I am the one.
4.note: tn Heb “I, the Lord, [am with] the first, and with the last ones I [am] he.”

5.anchor: the whole earth
5.note: tn Heb “the ends of the earth,” but this is a merism, where the earth’s extremities stand for its entirety, i.e., the extremities and everything in between them.

6.note: tn Heb “each his neighbor helps”; NCV “The workers help each other.”

7.anchor: He approves the quality of the welding,
7.note: tn Heb “saying of the welding, ‘It is good.’”

8.note: tn Or perhaps, “covenantal partner” (see 1 Kgs 5:15 HT [5:1 ET]; 2 Chr 20:7).

9.anchor: you whom I am bringing back
9.note: tn Heb “whom I have taken hold of [i.e., to lead back].”

10.anchor: yes, I uphold you with my saving right hand!
10.note: tn The “right hand” is a symbol of the Lord’s power to deliver (Exod 15:6, 12) and protect (Ps 63:9 HT [63:8 ET]). Here צֶדֶק (tsedeq) has its well-attested nuance of “vindicated righteousness,” i.e., “victory, deliverance” (see 45:8; 51:5, and BDB 841-42 s.v.).

11.anchor: your adversaries
11.note: tn Heb “the men of your strife”; NASB “those who contend with you.”

11.anchor: will be reduced to nothing
11.note: tn Heb “like nothing”; NAB “come to nought.”

12.anchor: your enemies
12.note: tn Heb “the men of your battle”; NAB “who do battle with you.”

14.note: tn Heb “your kinsman redeemer.” A גָּאַל (ga’al, “kinsman redeemer”) was a protector of the extended family’s interests.
sn See the note on the phrase “the Holy One of Israel” in 1:4.

15.anchor: you will make the hills like straw.
15.note: sn The mountains and hills symbolize hostile nations that are obstacles to Israel’s restoration.

17.anchor: , will respond to their prayers;
17.note: tn Heb “will answer them” (so ASV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT).

20.anchor: and that the Holy One of Israel has brought it into being.”
20.note: tn Or “created it” (KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV); TEV “has made it happen.”

21.anchor: “Produce your evidence,”
21.note: tn Heb “strong [words],” see HALOT 870 s.v. *עֲצֻמוֹת.

21.anchor: says Jacob’s king.
21.note: sn Apparently this challenge is addressed to the pagan idol gods, see vv. 23-24.

22.anchor: so we may examine them
22.note: tn Heb “so we might set [them to] our heart.”

22.anchor: and see how they were fulfilled.
22.note: tn Heb “and might know their outcome.”

23.anchor: so we might be frightened and in awe.
23.note: tc The translation assumes the Qere (וְנִרְאֶה [vÿnir’eh], from יָרֵא [yare’], “be afraid”).tn Heb “so we might be frightened and afraid together.” On the meaning of the verb שָׁתָע (shata’), see the note at v. 10.

24.anchor: the one who chooses to worship you is disgusting.
24.note: tn Heb “an object of disgust [is he who] chooses you.”

25.anchor: He steps on
25.note: tn The Hebrew text has וְיָבֹא (vÿyavo’, “and he comes”), but this is likely a corruption of an original וַיָּבָס (vayyavas), from בּוּס (bus, “step on”).

26.note: tn The words “who announced it” are supplied in the translation for clarification. The interrogative particle and verb are understood by ellipsis (see the preceding line).

27.anchor: I sent a herald to Jerusalem.
27.note: map For location see Map5-B1; Map6-F3; Map7-E2; Map8-F2; Map10-B3; JP1-F4; JP2-F4; JP3-F4; JP4-F4.

29.anchor: their metal images lack any real substance.
29.note: tn Heb “their statues are wind and nothing”; NASB “wind and emptiness”; NIV “wind and confusion.”

1.anchor: Your heavy images are burdensome to tired animals.
1.note: tn Heb “your loads are carried [as] a burden by a weary [animal].”
