2.note: tn נַפְשָׁם (nafsham, “their souls/lives”) is equivalent here to a third masculine plural suffix, but the third feminine singular verb הָלָכָה (halakhah, “they go”) agrees with the feminine noun נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh, “soul, life”).
sn The downfall of Babylon is depicted here. The idols are carried off by the victorious enemy; the gods are likened to defeated captives who cower before the enemy and are taken into exile.

3.anchor: you who have been supported from the time you left the womb.
3.note: tn Heb “who have been lifted up from the womb.”

4.anchor: I will carry you and rescue you.
4.note: sn Unlike the weary idol gods, whose images must be carried by animals, the Lord carries his weary people.

6.anchor: and weigh out silver on the scale
6.note: tn Heb “the reed,” probably referring to the beam of a scales. See BDB 889 s.v. קָנֶה 4.c.

7.anchor: it does not
7.note: tn Or perhaps, “cannot,” here and in the following two lines. The imperfect forms can indicate capability.

8.note: tn Heb “return [it], rebels, to heart”; NRSV “recall it to mind, you transgressors.”

9.anchor: Truly I am God, I have no peer;
9.note: tn Heb “and there is no other” (so NASB, NIV, NRSV).

10.anchor: and reveals beforehand
10.note: tn Or “from long ago”; KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV “from ancient times.”

11.anchor: Yes, I have decreed,
11.note: tn Heb “spoken”; KJV “I have spoken it.”

12.anchor: you who distance yourself from doing what is right.
12.note: tn Heb “who are far from righteousness [or perhaps, “deliverance”].”

13.anchor: I will adorn Israel with my splendor.”
13.note: tn Heb “to Israel my splendor”; KJV, ASV “for Israel my glory.”

1.note: tn Heb “and the belts of kings I will loosen”; NRSV “strip kings of their robes”; NIV “strip kings of their armor.”

2.note: tn That is, on the gates. Cf. CEV “break the iron bars on bronze gates.”

3.anchor: I will give you hidden treasures,
3.note: tn Heb “treasures of darkness” (KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV); TEV “treasures from dark, secret places.”

4.anchor: and give you a title of respect, even though you do not recognize
4.note: tn Or “know” (NCV, NRSV, TEV, NLT); NIV “acknowledge.”

5.anchor: I arm you for battle,
5.note: tn Heb “gird you” (so NASB) or “strengthen you” (so NIV).

5.anchor: even though you do not recognize
5.note: tn Or “know” (NAB, NCV, NRSV, TEV, CEV, NLT); NIV “have not acknowledged.”

6.note: tn The words “I do this” are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.
tn Heb “they” (so KJV, ASV); TEV, CEV “everyone”; NLT “all the world.”

7.anchor: and creates calamity.
7.note: sn This verses affirms that God is ultimately sovereign over his world, including mankind and nations. In accordance with his sovereign will, he can cause wars to cease and peace to predominate (as he was about to do for his exiled people through Cyrus), or he can bring disaster and judgment on nations (as he was about to do to Babylon through Cyrus).

8.note: tn The masculine singular pronominal suffix probably refers back to יָשַׁע (yasha’, “salvation”).

9.note: tn Heb “your work, there are no hands for it,” i.e., “your work looks like something made by a person who has no hands.”

10.anchor: “What in the world are you bringing forth?”
10.note: sn Verses 9-10 may allude to the exiles’ criticism that the Lord does not appear to know what he is doing.

11.anchor: How dare you tell me what to do with
11.note: tn Heb “Do you command me about…?” The rhetorical question sarcastically expresses the Lord’s disgust with those who question his ways.

12.anchor: I give orders to all the heavenly lights.
12.note: tn Heb “and to all their host I commanded.” See the notes at 40:26.

13.anchor: It is me – I stir him up and commission him;
13.note: tn Heb “I stir him up in righteousness”; NASB “I have aroused him.” See the note at 41:2. Cyrus (cf. 44:28) is in view here.

14.note: tn Or perhaps, “among.” Cf. KJV, ASV “Surely God is in thee.”
tn Heb “there is no other” (so NIV, NRSV). The same phrase occurs at the end of v. 18, in v. 21, and at the end of v. 22.

16.anchor: those who fashion idols will all be humiliated.
16.note: tn “together they will walk in humiliation, the makers of images.”

17.anchor: you will never again be ashamed or humiliated.
17.note: tn Heb “you will not be ashamed and you will not be humiliated for ages of future time.”

18.anchor: he did not create it without order,
18.note: tn Or “unformed.” Gen 1:2 describes the world as “unformed” (תֹהוּ, tohu) prior to God’s creative work, but God then formed the world and made it fit for habitation.

19.anchor: who makes reliable announcements.
19.note: tn The translation above assumes that צֶדֶק (tsedeq) and מֵישָׁרִים (mesharim) are adverbial accusatives (see 33:15). If they are taken as direct objects, indicating the content of what is spoken, one might translate, “who proclaims deliverance, who announces justice.”

21.anchor: a God who vindicates and delivers;
21.note: tn Or “a righteous God and deliverer”; NASB, NIV, NRSV “a righteous God and a Savior.”

22.anchor: Turn to me so you can be delivered,
22.note: tn The Niphal imperative with prefixed vav (ו) indicates purpose after the preceding imperative. The Niphal probably has a tolerative sense, “allow yourselves to be delivered, accept help.”

23.anchor: every tongue will solemnly affirm;
23.note: tn Heb “swear” (so KJV, NAB, NIV, NRSV); NLT “confess allegiance.”

24.anchor: All who are angry at him will cower before him.
24.note: tn Heb “will come to him and be ashamed.”

25.anchor: and will boast in him.
25.note: tn Heb “In the Lord all the offspring of Israel will be vindicated and boast.”

1.anchor: In those days Hezekiah was stricken with a terminal illness.
1.note: tn Heb “was sick to the point of dying”; NRSV “became sick and was at the point of death.”

3.anchor: . Remember how I have served you
3.note: tn Heb “walked before you.” For a helpful discussion of the background and meaning of this Hebrew idiom, see M. Cogan and H. Tadmor, II Kings (AB), 254.

3.anchor: faithfully and with wholehearted devotion,
3.note: tn Heb “and with a complete heart”; KJV, ASV “with a perfect heart.”

3.anchor: and how I have carried out your will.”
3.note: tn Heb “and that which is good in your eyes I have done.”

3.anchor: Then Hezekiah wept bitterly.
3.note: tn Heb “wept with great weeping”; NCV “cried loudly”; TEV “began to cry bitterly.”

4.anchor: told Isaiah,
4.note: tn Heb “and the word of the Lord came to Isaiah, saying.”

5.anchor: God of your ancestor
5.note: tn Heb “father” (so KJV, NAB, NIV).

21.note: tc If original to Isaiah 38, vv. 21-22 have obviously been misplaced in the course of the text’s transmission, and would most naturally be placed here, between Isa 38:6 and 38:7. See 2 Kgs 20:7-8, where these verses are placed at this point in the narrative, not at the end. Another possibility is that these verses were not in the original account, and a scribe, familiar with the 2 Kgs version of the story, appended vv. 21-22 to the end of the account in Isaiah 38.

7.anchor: Isaiah replied,
7.note: tn The words “Isaiah replied” are supplied in the translation for clarification. In the present form of the Hebrew text v. 7 is joined directly to v. 6, but vv. 21-22, if original to Isaiah 38, must be inserted here. See 2 Kgs 20:7-8.

8.anchor: Look, I will make the shadow go back ten steps on the stairs of Ahaz.”
8.note: tn Heb “the shadow on the steps which [the sun] had gone down, on the steps of Ahaz, with the sun, back ten steps.”sn These steps probably functioned as a type of sundial. See HALOT 614 s.v. מַעֲלָה and M. Cogan and H. Tadmor, II Kings (AB), 256.

8.anchor: And then the shadow went back ten steps.
8.note: tn Heb “and the sun returned ten steps on the steps which it had gone down.”

10.anchor: I am deprived
10.note: tn The precise meaning of the verb is uncertain. The Pual of of פָּקַד (paqad) occurs only here and in Exod 38:21, where it appears to mean “passed in review” or “mustered.” Perhaps the idea is, “I have been called away for the remainder of my years.” To bring out the sense more clearly, one can translate, “I am deprived of the rest of my years.”

11.anchor: I will no longer look on humankind with the inhabitants of the world.
11.note: tc The Hebrew text has חָדֶל (khadel), which appears to be derived from a verbal root meaning “to cease, refrain.” But the form has probably suffered an error of transmission; the original form (attested in a few medieval Hebrew mss) was likely חֶלֶד (kheled, “world”).

12.anchor: You turn day into night and end my life.
12.note: tn Heb “from day to night you bring me to an end.”

13.anchor: you turn day into night and end my life.
13.note: tn Heb “from day to night you bring me to an end.”

14.note: tn Heb “stand surety for me.” Hezekiah seems to be picturing himself as a debtor who is being exploited; he asks that the Lord might relieve his debt and deliver him from the oppressive creditor.

15.anchor: I will walk slowly all my years because I am overcome with grief.
15.note: tn Heb “because of the bitterness of my soul.”

16.anchor: Restore my health
16.note: tn The prefixed verbal form could be taken as indicative, “you restore my health,” but the following imperatival form suggests it be understood as an imperfect of request.

17.anchor: For you removed all my sins from your sight.
17.note: tn Heb “for you threw behind your back all my sins.”

18.anchor: death does not
18.note: tn The negative particle is understood by ellipsis in this line. See GKC 483 §152.z.

20.anchor: ’s temple.”
20.note: tn Heb “all the days of our lives in the house of the Lord.”sn Note that vv. 21-22 have been placed between vv. 6-7, where they logically belong. See 2 Kgs 20:7-8.

1.anchor: he commissioned me when my mother brought me into the world.
1.note: tn Heb “from the inner parts of my mother he mentioned my name.”

2.anchor: he hid me in his quiver.
2.note: sn The figurative language emphasizes the servant’s importance as the Lord’s effective instrument. The servant’s mouth, which stands metonymically for his words, is compared to a sharp sword because he will be an effective spokesman on God’s behalf (see 50:4). The Lord holds his hand on the servant, ready to draw and use him at the appropriate time. The servant is like a sharpened arrow reserved in a quiver for just the right moment.

3.anchor: Israel, through whom I will reveal my splendor.”
3.note: sn This verse identifies the servant as Israel. This seems to refer to the exiled nation (cf. 41:8-9; 44:1-2, 21; 45:4; 48:20), but in vv. 5-6 this servant says he has been commissioned to reconcile Israel to God, so he must be distinct from the exiled nation. This servant is an ideal “Israel” who, like Moses of old, mediates a covenant for the nation (see v. 8), leads them out of bondage (v. 9a), and carries out God’s original plan for Israel by positively impacting the pagan nations (see v. 6b). By living according to God’s law, Israel was to be a model of God’s standards of justice to the surrounding nations (Deut 4:6-8). The sinful nation failed, but the servant, the ideal “Israel,” will succeed by establishing justice throughout the earth.

4.anchor: my God will reward me.
4.note: tn Heb “But my justice is with the Lord, and my reward [or “wage”] with my God.”

5.anchor: for my God is my source of strength
5.note: tn Heb “and my God is [perhaps, “having been”] my strength.” The disjunctive structure (vav [ו] + subject + verb) is interpreted here as indicating a causal circumstantial clause.

6.note: tn Heb “be” (so KJV, ASV); CEV “you must take.”

7.anchor: “Kings will see and rise in respect,
7.note: tn For this sense of קוּם (qum), see Gen 19:1; 23:7; 33:10; Lev 19:32; 1 Sam 20:41; 25:41; 1 Kgs 2:19; Job 29:8.

8.note: tn The Hiphil of קוּם (qum, “arise”) is probably used here in the sense of “rebuild.”
tn The “land” probably stands by metonymy for the ruins within it.

9.anchor: and to those who are in dark dungeons,
9.note: tn Heb “in darkness” (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV); NLT “the prisoners of darkness.”
tn Heb “show yourselves” (so ASV, NAB, NASB).

10.anchor: the sun’s oppressive heat will not beat down on them,
10.note: tn Heb “and the heat and the sun will not strike them.” In Isa 35:7, its only other occurrence in the OT, שָׁרָב (sharav) stands parallel to “parched ground” and in contrast to “pool.” In later Hebrew and Aramaic it refers to “dry heat, heat of the sun” (Jastrow 1627 s.v.). Here it likely has this nuance and forms a hendiadys with “sun.”

12.anchor: and others from the land of Sinim!
12.note: tc The MT reads “Sinim” here; the Dead Sea Scrolls read “Syene,” a location in Egypt associated with modern Aswan. A number of recent translations adopt this reading: “Syene” (NAB, NRSV); “Aswan” (NIV); “Egypt” (NLT).sn The precise location of the land of Sinim is uncertain, but since the north and west are mentioned in the previous line, it was a probably located in the distant east or south.

13.anchor: and shows compassion to the
13.note: tn Heb “his” (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV).

14.note: tn The Hebrew term translated “sovereign master” here is אֲדֹנָי (’adonay).

15.anchor: I could never forget you!
15.note: sn The argument of v. 15 seems to develop as follows: The Lord has an innate attachment to Zion, just like a mother does for her infant child. But even if mothers were to suddenly abandon their children, the Lord would never forsake Zion. In other words, the Lord’s attachment to Zion is like a mother’s attachment to her infant child, but even stronger.

16.anchor: Look, I have inscribed your name
16.note: tn Heb “you.” Here the pronoun is put by metonymy for the person’s name.

18.anchor: Look all around you!
18.note: tn Heb “Lift up around your eyes and see.”

19.anchor: it is desolate and devastated.
19.note: tn Heb “Indeed your ruins and your desolate places, and the land of your destruction.” This statement is abruptly terminated in the Hebrew text and left incomplete.

20.anchor: make room for us so we can live here.’
20.note: tn Heb “draw near to me so I can dwell.”

21.anchor: dismissed and divorced.
21.note: tn Or “exiled and thrust away”; NIV “exiled and rejected.”

23.anchor: and they will lick the dirt on
23.note: tn Or “at your feet” (NAB, NIV); NLT “from your feet.”

24.anchor: or captives be rescued from a conqueror?
24.note: tc The Hebrew text has צָדִיק (tsadiq, “a righteous [one]”), but this makes no sense in the parallelism. The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa reads correctly עריץ (“violent [one], tyrant”; see v. 25).

26.note: tn Heb “your redeemer.” See the note at 41:14.
tn Heb “the powerful [one] of Jacob.” See 1:24.

1.anchor: but not in an honest and just manner.
1.note: tn Heb “not in truth and not in righteousness.”

2.note: tn Heb “lean on” (so NASB, NRSV); NAB, NIV “rely on.”

3.anchor: I issued the decrees and made the predictions;
3.note: tn Heb “and from my mouth they came forth and I caused them to be heard.”

4.anchor: and your forehead like bronze.
4.note: sn The image is that of a person who has tensed the muscles of the face and neck as a sign of resolute refusal.

6.anchor: that are previously unrevealed and you do not know about.
6.note: tn Heb “and hidden things, and you do not know them.”

7.anchor: ‘Yes,
7.note: tn Heb “look”; KJV, NASB “Behold.”

8.note: tn Or “called” (KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV).

9.anchor: for the sake of my prestige
9.note: tn Heb “and my praise.” לְמַעַן (lÿma’an, “for the sake of”) is understood by ellipsis (note the preceding line).

9.anchor: I restrain myself from destroying you.
9.note: tn Heb “I restrain [myself] concerning you not to cut you off.”

10.anchor: I have purified you
10.note: tc The Hebrew text has בְּחַרְתִּיךָ (bÿkhartikha, “I have chosen you”), but the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa reads correctly בחנתיכה (“I have tested you”). The metallurgical background of the imagery suggests that purification through testing is the idea.

11.anchor: I will not share my glory with anyone else!
11.note: sn See 42:8.

12.anchor: and at the very end.
12.note: tn Heb “I [am] he, I [am the] first, also I [am the] last.”

14.anchor: he will exert his power against the Babylonians.
14.note: tn Heb “and his arm [against] the Babylonians.”

15.anchor: I lead him and he will succeed.
15.note: tn Heb “and his way will be prosperous.”

16.anchor: has sent me, accompanied by his spirit.
16.note: sn The speaker here is not identified specifically, but he is probably Cyrus, the Lord’s “ally” mentioned in vv. 14-15.

17.anchor: the Holy One of Israel:
17.note: sn See the note on the phrase “the Holy One of Israel” in 1:4.

18.anchor: deliverance would have come to you like the waves of the sea.
18.note: tn Heb “and your righteousness like the waves of the sea.” צְדָקָה (tsÿdaqah) probably refers here to divine deliverance from enemies. See v. 19.

19.anchor: and eliminated from my presence.
19.note: tn Heb “and his name would not be cut off and would not be destroyed from before me.”

20.anchor: protects
20.note: tn Heb “redeems.” See the note at 41:14.

21.anchor: he splits open a rock and water flows out.’
21.note: sn The translation above (present tense) assumes that this verse describes God’s provision for returning Babylonian exiles (see v. 20; 35:6; 49:10) in terms reminiscent of the Exodus from Egypt (see Exod 17:6).

1.anchor: because of your rebellious acts I divorced your mother.
1.note: sn The Lord admits he did divorce Zion, but that too was the result of the nation’s sins. The force of the earlier rhetorical question comes into clearer focus now. The question does not imply that a certificate does not exist and that no divorce occurred. Rather, the question asks for the certificate to be produced so the accuser can see the reason for the divorce in black and white. The Lord did not put Zion away arbitrarily.

2.note: tn Heb “the fish stink from lack of water and die from thirst.”

4.anchor: he makes me alert so I can listen attentively as disciples do.
4.note: tn Heb “he arouses for me an ear, to hear like disciples.”

5.anchor: has spoken to me clearly;
5.note: tn Or perhaps, “makes me obedient.” The text reads literally, “has opened for me an ear.”

6.anchor: I offered my back to those who attacked,
6.note: tn Or perhaps, “who beat [me].”

7.anchor: For that reason I am steadfastly resolved;
7.note: tn Heb “Therefore I set my face like flint.”

8.note: tn Heb “Who is the master of my judgment?”
tn Heb “let him approach me”; NAB, NIV “Let him confront me.”

10.anchor: Whoever walks in deep darkness,
10.note: tn The plural indicates degree. Darkness may refer to exile and/or moral evil.

11.anchor: you will lie down in a place of pain.
11.note: sn The imagery may be that of a person who becomes ill and is forced to lie down in pain on a sickbed. Some see this as an allusion to a fiery place of damnation because of the imagery employed earlier in the verse.

1.note: tn The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on “in the future” in 2:2.

2.anchor: Eliakim the palace supervisor, Shebna the scribe, and the leading priests,
2.note: tn Heb “elders of the priests” (so KJV, NAB, NASB); NCV “the older priests”; NRSV, TEV, CEV “the senior priests.”

3.anchor: “This is what Hezekiah says:
3.note: tn In the Hebrew text this verse begins with “they said to him” (cf. NRSV).

3.anchor: ‘This is a day of distress, insults,
3.note: tn Or “rebuke” (KJV, NAB, NIV, NRSV), or “correction.”

3.anchor: and humiliation,
3.note: tn Or “contempt”; NAB, NIV, NRSV “disgrace.”

3.anchor: as when a baby is ready to leave the birth canal, but the mother lacks the strength to push it through.
3.note: tn Heb “when sons come to the cervical opening and there is no strength to give birth.”

4.anchor: your God will hear all these things the chief adviser has spoken on behalf of his master, the king of Assyria, who sent him to taunt the living God.
4.note: tn Heb “all the words of the chief adviser whom his master, the king of Assyria, sent to taunt the living God.”

4.anchor: your God hears, perhaps he will punish him for the things he has said.
4.note: tn Heb “and rebuke the words which the Lord your God hears.”

4.anchor: So pray for this remnant that remains.’”
4.note: tn Heb “and lift up a prayer on behalf of the remnant that is found.”

6.anchor: says: “Don’t be afraid because of the things you have heard – these insults the king of Assyria’s servants have hurled against me.
6.note: tn Heb “by which the servants of the king of Assyria have insulted me.”

7.anchor: Look, I will take control of his mind;
7.note: tn Heb “I will put in him a spirit.” The precise sense of רוּחַ (ruakh, “spirit”) is uncertain in this context. It may refer to a spiritual being who will take control of his mind (see 1 Kgs 22:19), or it could refer to a disposition of concern and fear. In either case the Lord’s sovereignty over the king is apparent.

7.anchor: he will receive a report and return to his own land. I will cut him down
7.note: tn Heb “cause him to fall” (so KJV, ASV, NAB), that is, “kill him.”

8.anchor: When the chief adviser heard the king of Assyria had departed from Lachish, he left and went to Libnah, where the king was campaigning.
8.note: tn Heb “and the chief adviser returned and he found the king of Assyria fighting against Libnah, for he heard that he had departed from Lachish.”

9.note: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the king) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

9.anchor: heard that King Tirhakah of Ethiopia
9.note: tn Heb “Cush” (so NASB); NIV, NCV “the Cushite king of Egypt.”

9.anchor: was marching out to fight him.
9.note: tn Heb “heard concerning Tirhakah king of Cush, ‘He has come out to fight with you.’”
tn The Hebrew text has, “and he heard and he sent,” but the parallel in 2 Kgs 19:9 has וַיָּשָׁב וַיִּשְׁלַח (vayyashav vayyishlakh, “and he returned and he sent”), i.e., “he again sent.”

11.anchor: Certainly you have heard how the kings of Assyria have annihilated all lands.
11.note: tn Heb “Look, you have heard what the kings of Assyria have done to all the lands, annihilating them.”

11.anchor: Do you really think you will be rescued?
11.note: tn Heb “and will you be rescued?” The rhetorical question expects the answer, “No, of course not!”

12.anchor: Were the nations whom my predecessors
12.note: tn Heb “fathers” (so KJV, NAB, NASB); NIV “forefathers”; NCV “ancestors.”

12.anchor: destroyed – the nations of Gozan, Haran, Rezeph, and the people of Eden in Telassar – rescued by their gods?
12.note: tn Heb “Did the gods of the nations whom my fathers destroyed rescue them – Gozan and Haran, and Rezeph and the sons of Eden who are in Telassar?”

13.anchor: Where are the king of Hamath, the king of Arpad, and the kings of Lair,
13.note: sn Lair was a city located in northeastern Babylon. See M. Cogan and H. Tadmor, II Kings (AB), 235.

14.note: tc The Hebrew text has the plural, “letters.” The final mem (ם) may be dittographic (note the initial mem on the form that immediately follows). Some Greek and Aramaic witnesses have the singular. If so, one still has to deal with the yod that is part of the plural ending. J. N. Oswalt refers to various commentators who have suggested ways to understand the plural form (Isaiah [NICOT], 1:652).

14.anchor: from the messengers and read it.
14.note: tn In the parallel text in 2 Kgs 19:14 the verb has the plural suffix, “them,” but this probably reflects a later harmonization to the preceding textual corruption (of “letter” to “letters”).

16.anchor: who commands armies, O God of Israel, who is enthroned on the cherubim!
16.note: sn Cherubim (singular “cherub”) refers to the images of winged angelic creatures that were above the ark of the covenant.

16.anchor: You alone are God over all the kingdoms of the earth. You made the sky
16.note: tn Or “the heavens.” The Hebrew term שָׁמַיִם (shamayim) may be translated “heavens” or “sky” depending on the context.

17.anchor: , and observe! Listen to this entire message Sennacherib sent and how he taunts the living God!
17.note: tn Heb “Hear all the words of Sennacherib which he sent to taunt the living God.”

18.anchor: , that the kings of Assyria have destroyed all the nations
18.note: tn The Hebrew text here has “all the lands,” but the parallel text in 2 Kgs 19:17 has “the nations.”

19.anchor: They have burned the gods of the nations,
19.note: tn Heb “and they put their gods in the fire.”

19.anchor: for they are not really gods, but only the product of human hands manufactured from wood and stone. That is why the Assyrians could destroy them.
19.note: tn Heb “so they destroyed them” (NASB similar).

20.note: tn The parallel text in 2 Kgs 19:19 reads, “that you, Lord, are the only God.”

21.anchor: God of Israel says: ‘Because you prayed to me concerning King Sennacherib of Assyria,
21.note: tn The parallel text in 2 Kgs 19:20 reads, “That which you prayed to me concerning Sennacherib king of Assyria I have heard.” The verb “I have heard” does not appear in Isa 37:21, where אֲשֶׁר (’asher) probably has a causal sense: “because.”

22.anchor: shakes her head after you.
22.note: sn Shaking the head was a mocking gesture of derision.

23.anchor: At the Holy One of Israel!
23.note: sn See the note on the phrase “the Holy One of Israel” in 1:4.

24.anchor: I invaded its most remote regions,
24.note: tn Heb “the height of its extremity”; ASV “its farthest height.”

25.anchor: and drank water.
25.note: tc The Hebrew text has simply, “I dug and drank water.” But the parallel text in 2 Kgs 19:24 has “foreign waters.” זָרִים (zarim, “foreign”) may have accidentally dropped out of the Isaianic text by homoioteleuton (cf. NCV, NIV, NLT). Note that the preceding word, מַיִם (mayim, “water) also ends in mem (ם). The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa has “foreign waters” for this line. However, in several other passages the 1QIsaa scroll harmonizes with 2 Kgs 19 against the MT (Isa 36:5; 37:9, 20). Since the addition of “foreign” to this text in Isaiah by a later scribe would be more likely than its deletion, the MT reading should be accepted.

26.note: tn Heb “and it is to cause to crash into heaps of ruins fortified cities.” The subject of the third feminine singular verb תְהִי (tÿhi) is the implied plan, referred to in the preceding lines with third feminine singular pronominal suffixes.

27.anchor: when it is scorched by the east wind.
27.note: tc The Hebrew text has “scorched before the standing grain” (perhaps meaning “before it reaches maturity”), but it is preferable to emend קָמָה (qamah, “standing grain”) to קָדִים (qadim, “east wind”) with the support of 1Q Isaa; cf. J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:657, n. 8.

28.anchor: and how you rage against me.
28.note: tc Heb “your going out and your coming in and how you have raged against me.” Several scholars have suggested that this line is probably dittographic (note the beginning of the next line). However, most English translations include the statement in question at the end of v. 28 and the beginning of v. 29. Interestingly, the LXX does not have this clause at the end of v. 28 and the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa does not have it at the beginning of v. 29. In light of this ambiguous manuscript evidence, it appears best to retain the clause in both verses.

29.anchor: I will put my hook in your nose,
29.note: sn The word-picture has a parallel in Assyrian sculpture. See M. Cogan and H. Tadmor, II Kings (AB), 238.

30.note: tn At this point the word concerning the king of Assyria (vv. 22-29) ends and the Lord again addresses Hezekiah and the people directly (see v. 21).

30.anchor: “This will be your reminder that I have spoken the truth:
30.note: tn Heb “and this is your sign.” In this case the אוֹת (’ot, “sign”) is a future reminder of God’s intervention designated before the actual intervention takes place. For similar “signs” see Exod 3:12 and Isa 7:14-25.

30.anchor: This year you will eat what grows wild,
30.note: sn This refers to crops that grew up on their own (that is, without cultivation) from the seed planted in past years.
tn Heb “and in the second year” (so ASV).

30.anchor: what grows on its own. But the year after that
30.note: tn Heb “in the third year” (so KJV, NAB).

30.anchor: you will plant seed and harvest crops; you will plant vines and consume their produce.
30.note: tn The four plural imperatival verb forms in v. 30b are used rhetorically. The Lord commands the people to plant, harvest, etc. to emphasize the certainty of restored peace and prosperity.

31.anchor: Those who remain in Judah will take root in the ground and bear fruit.
31.note: tn Heb “The remnant of the house of Judah that is left will add roots below and produce fruit above.”

32.note: tn Heb “the zeal of the Lord who commands armies [traditionally, the Lord of hosts].” In this context the Lord’s “zeal” refers to his intense devotion to and love for his people which prompts him to protect and restore them.

33.anchor: He will not attack it with his shielded warriors,
33.note: tn Heb “[with] a shield” (so ASV, NASB, NRSV).

35.anchor: I will shield this city and rescue it for the sake of my reputation and because of my promise to David my servant.”’”
35.note: tn Heb “for my sake and for the sake of David my servant.”

36.note: tn Traditionally, “the angel of the Lord” (so NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT).
tn The word “troops” is supplied in the translation for smoothness and clarity.

36.anchor: in the Assyrian camp. When they
36.note: tn This refers to the Israelites and/or the rest of the Assyrian army.

36.anchor: got up early the next morning, there were all the corpses!
36.note: tn Heb “look, all of them were dead bodies”; NLT “they found corpses everywhere.”

37.anchor: So King Sennacherib of Assyria broke camp and went on his way. He went home and stayed in Nineveh.
37.note: tn Heb “and Sennacherib king of Assyria departed and went and returned and lived in Nineveh.”

38.note: sn The assassination of King Sennacherib probably took place in 681 b.c.
tn The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on “in the future” in 2:2.

38.anchor: in the temple of his god Nisroch,
38.note: sn No such Mesopotamian god is presently known. Perhaps the name Nisroch is a corruption of Nusku.

38.anchor: his sons Adrammelech and Sharezer struck him down with the sword.
38.note: sn Extra-biblical sources also mention the assassination of Sennacherib, though they refer to only one assassin. See M. Cogan and H. Tadmor, II Kings (AB), 239-40.

1.note: map For location see Map5-B1; Map6-F3; Map7-E2; Map8-F2; Map10-B3; JP1-F4; JP2-F4; JP3-F4; JP4-F4.

2.note: tc The Hebrew text has שְּׂבִי (shÿvi), which some understand as a feminine singular imperative from יָשַׁב (yashav, “sit”). The LXX, Vulgate, Syriac, and the Targum support the MT reading (the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa does indirectly). Some interpret this to mean “take your throne”: The Lord exhorts Jerusalem to get up from the dirt and sit, probably with the idea of sitting in a place of honor (J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah [NICOT], 2:361). However, the form is likely a corruption of שְׁבִיָּה (shÿviyyah, “captive”), which appears in the parallel line.

5.anchor: “and my name is constantly slandered
5.note: tn The verb is apparently a Hitpolal form (with assimilated tav, ת) from the root נָאַץ (na’ats), but GKC 151-52 §55.b explains it as a mixed form, combining Pual and Hitpolel readings.

6.anchor: for this reason they will know
6.note: tn The verb is understood by ellipsis (note the preceding line).
tn Heb “in that day” (so KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV).

7.anchor: who says to Zion, “Your God reigns!”
7.note: tn Or “has become king.” When a new king was enthroned, his followers would give this shout. For other examples of this enthronement formula (Qal perfect 3rd person masculine singular מָלַךְ [malakh], followed by the name of the king), see 2 Sam 15:10; 1 Kgs 1:11, 13, 18; 2 Kgs 9:13. The Lord is an eternal king, but here he is pictured as a victorious warrior who establishes his rule from Zion.

8.anchor: for they see with their very own eyes
8.note: tn Heb “eye in eye”; KJV, ASV “eye to eye”; NAB “directly, before their eyes.”

9.note: tn Or “redeems.” See the note at 41:14.

10.anchor: our God deliver.
10.note: tn Heb “the deliverance of our God.” “God” is a subjective genitive here.

11.anchor: ’s holy items!
11.note: tn Heb “the vessels of the Lord” (so KJV, NAB).

12.anchor: or leave in a panic.
12.note: tn Heb “or go in flight”; NAB “leave in headlong flight.”

13.anchor: He will be elevated, lifted high, and greatly exalted
13.note: tn This piling up of synonyms emphasizes the degree of the servant’s coming exaltation.

14.note: tn Heb “such was the disfigurement.” The noun מִשְׁחַת (mishkhat) occurs only here. It may be derived from the verbal root שָׁחַת (shakhat, “be ruined”; see BDB 1007-8 s.v. שָׁחַת). The construct form appears here before a prepositional phrase (cf. GKC 421 §130.a).

14.anchor: he no longer looked like a man;
14.note: tn Heb “from a man his appearance.” The preposition מִן (min) here carries the sense “away from,” i.e., “so as not to be.” See BDB 583 s.v.

15.anchor: Kings will be shocked by his exaltation,
15.note: tn Heb “Because of him kings will shut their mouths,” i.e., be speechless.

1.note: tn Or “For” (NASB, NRSV).

3.anchor: I will not have pity on anyone,”
3.note: tn Heb “I will not meet a man.” The verb פָּגַע (pagah) apparently carries the nuance “meet with kindness” here (cf. 64:5, and see BDB 803 s.v. Qal.2).

4.anchor: the Holy One of Israel.
4.note: tc The Hebrew text reads, “Our redeemer – the Lord who commands armies [traditionally, the Lord of hosts] is his name, the Holy One of Israel.” The ancient Greek version adds “says” before “our redeemer.” אָמַר (’amar) may have accidentally dropped from the text by virtual haplography. Note that the preceding word אָדָם (’adam) is graphically similar.sn See the note on the phrase “the Holy One of Israel” in 1:4.

5.anchor: Indeed,
5.note: tn Or “For” (NASB, NRSV).

6.anchor: you even placed a very heavy burden on old people.
6.note: tn Heb “on the old you made very heavy your yoke.”

7.anchor: you did not consider how it would turn out.
7.note: tn Heb “you did not remember its outcome”; NAB “you disregarded their outcome.”

8.anchor: I will never lose my children.’
8.note: tn Heb “I will not live [as] a widow, and I will not know loss of children.”

9.note: sn Reference is made to incantations and amulets, both of which were important in Mesopotamian religion. They were used to ward off danger and demons.

10.anchor: when you say, ‘I am unique! No one can compare to me!’
10.note: tn See the note at v. 8.

11.anchor: before you recognize it.
11.note: tn Heb “you will not know”; NIV “you cannot foresee.”

12.anchor: maybe you will scare away disaster.
12.note: tn Heb “maybe you will cause to tremble.” The object “disaster” is supplied in the translation for clarification. See the note at v. 9.

13.anchor: let them rescue you from the disaster that is about to overtake you!
13.note: tn Heb “let them stand and rescue you – the ones who see omens in the sky, who gaze at the stars, who make known by months – from those things which are coming upon you.”

14.note: tn The Hebrew text reads literally, “there is no coal [for?] their food, light to sit before it.” Some emend לַחְמָם (lakhmam, “their food”) to לְחֻמָּם (lÿkhummam, “to warm them”; see HALOT 328 s.v. חמם). This statement may allude to Isa 44:16, where idolaters are depicted warming themselves over a fire made from wood, part of which was used to form idols. The fire of divine judgment will be no such campfire; its flames will devour and destroy.

15.anchor: Each strays off in his own direction,
15.note: tn Heb “each to his own side, they err.”

1.note: tn Heb “to whom” (so KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV).

tn Heb “the arm of the Lord.” The “arm of the Lord” is a metaphor of military power; it pictures the Lord as a warrior who bares his arm, takes up his weapon, and crushes his enemies (cf. 51:9-10; 63:5-6). But Israel had not seen the Lord’s military power at work in the servant.

2.anchor: no special appearance that we should want to follow him.
2.note: tn Heb “that we should desire him.” The vav conjunctive prefixed to the imperfect introduces a result clause here. See GKC 504-5 §166.a.

3.anchor: he was despised, and we considered him insignificant.
3.note: sn The servant is likened to a seriously ill person who is shunned by others because of his horrible disease.

4.anchor: attacked by God, and afflicted for something he had done.
4.note: tn The words “for something he had done” are supplied in the translation for clarification. The group now realizes he suffered because of his identification with them, not simply because he was a special target of divine anger.

5.anchor: because of his wounds we have been healed.
5.note: sn Continuing to utilize the imagery of physical illness, the group acknowledges that the servant’s willingness to carry their illnesses (v. 4) resulted in their being healed. Healing is a metaphor for forgiveness here.

6.anchor: caused the sin of all of us to attack him.
6.note: tn Elsewhere the Hiphil of פָגַע (paga’) means “to intercede verbally” (Jer 15:11; 36:25) or “to intervene militarily” (Isa 59:16), but neither nuance fits here. Apparently here the Hiphil is the causative of the normal Qal meaning, “encounter, meet, touch.” The Qal sometimes refers to a hostile encounter or attack; when used in this way the object is normally introduced by the preposition -בְּ (bet, see Josh 2:16; Judg 8:21; 15:12, etc.). Here the causative Hiphil has a double object – the Lord makes “sin” attack “him” (note that the object attacked is introduced by the preposition -בְּ. In their sin the group was like sheep who had wandered from God’s path. They were vulnerable to attack; the guilt of their sin was ready to attack and destroy them. But then the servant stepped in and took the full force of the attack.

7.anchor: he did not even open his mouth.
7.note: sn This verse emphasizes the servant’s silent submission. The comparison to a sheep does not necessarily suggest a sacrificial metaphor. Sheep were slaughtered for food as well as for sacrificial rituals, and טֶבַח (tevakh) need not refer to sacrificial slaughter (see Gen 43:16; Prov 7:22; 9:2; Jer 50:27; note also the use of the related verb in Exod 21:37; Deut 28:31; 1 Sam 25:11).

8.anchor: because of the rebellion of his own
8.note: tn The Hebrew text reads “my people,” a reading followed by most English versions, but this is problematic in a context where the first person plural predominates, and where God does not appear to speak again until v. 11b. Therefore, it is preferable to read with the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa עמו (“his people”). In this case, the group speaking in these verses is identified as the servant’s people (compare פְּשָׁעֵנוּ [pÿsha’enu, “our rebellious deeds”] in v. 5 with פֶּשַׁע עַמִּי [pesha’ ’ammi, “the rebellion of his people”] in v. 8).

9.note: tn If the second line is antithetical, then עַל (’al) is probably causal here, explaining why the servant was buried in a rich man’s tomb, rather than that of criminal. If the first two lines are synonymous, then עַל is probably concessive: “even though….”

10.anchor: he will see descendants and enjoy long life,
10.note: sn The idiomatic and stereotypical language emphasizes the servant’s restoration to divine favor. Having numerous descendants and living a long life are standard signs of divine blessing. See Job 42:13-16.

11.anchor: for he carried their sins.
11.note: tn The circumstantial clause (note the vav [ו] + object + subject + verb pattern) is understood as causal here. The prefixed verb form is either a preterite or an imperfect used in a customary manner.

12.anchor: and intervened
12.note: tn The Hiphil of פָּגַע (paga’) can mean “cause to attack” (v. 6), “urge, plead verbally” (Jer 15:11; 36:25), or “intervene militarily” (Isa 59:16). Perhaps the third nuance fits best here, for military imagery is employed in the first two lines of the verse.

2.note: tn Heb “your stakes strengthen.”

3.anchor: your children will conquer
3.note: tn Or “take possession of”; NAB “shall dispossess.”

4.anchor: you will no longer remember the disgrace of your abandonment.
4.note: tn Another option is to translate, “the disgrace of our widowhood” (so NRSV). However, the following context (vv. 6-7) refers to Zion’s husband, the Lord, abandoning her, not dying. This suggests that an אַלְמָנָה (’almanah) was a woman who had lost her husband, whether by death or abandonment.

5.anchor: He is your protector,
5.note: tn Or “redeemer.” See the note at 41:14.

5.anchor: the Holy One of Israel.
5.note: sn See the note on the phrase “the Holy One of Israel” in 1:4.

6.anchor: like a wife who has been abandoned and suffers from depression,
6.note: tn Heb “like a woman abandoned and grieved in spirit.”

7.anchor: “For a short time I abandoned
7.note: tn Or “forsook” (NASB).

8.note: tn Or “redeemer.” See the note at 41:14.

9.anchor: when I vowed that the waters of Noah’s flood
9.note: tn Heb “the waters of Noah” (so NAB, NIV, NRSV).

10.anchor: nor will my covenant of friendship
10.note: tn Heb “peace” (so many English versions); NLT “of blessing.”

11.anchor: “O afflicted one, driven away,
11.note: tn Or, more literally, “windblown, storm tossed.”

12.anchor: and your outer wall
12.note: tn Heb “border” (so ASV); NASB “your entire wall.”

12.anchor: out of beautiful
12.note: tn Heb “delightful”; KJV “pleasant.”

13.anchor: and your children will enjoy great prosperity.
13.note: tn Heb “and great [will be] the peace of your sons.”

14.note: tn Heb “it,” i.e., the “terror” just mentioned.

15.anchor: Whoever tries to challenge you will be defeated.
15.note: tn Heb “will fall over you.” The expression נָפַל עַל (nafal ’al) can mean “attack,” but here it means “fall over to,” i.e., “surrender to.”

16.anchor: and forges a weapon.
16.note: tn Heb “who brings out an implement for his work.”

17.anchor: I will vindicate them,”
17.note: tn Heb “this is the inheritance of the servants of the Lord, and their vindication from me.”

1.anchor: I am ready to vindicate you openly.
1.note: tn Heb “for near is my deliverance to enter, and my vindication [or “righteousness”] to be revealed.”

2.anchor: who refrain from doing anything that is wrong.
2.note: tn Heb and who keeps his hand from doing any evil.”

3.anchor: will certainly
3.note: tn The infinitive absolute precedes the finite verb for emphasis.

4.anchor: and are faithful to
4.note: tn Heb “and take hold of” (so KJV); NASB “hold fast.”

5.anchor: I will set up a permanent monument
5.note: tn Heb “name” (so KJV, NIV, NRSV).

6.note: tn Heb “and take hold of”; NAB “hold to”; NIV, NRSV “hold fast.”

7.anchor: for my temple will be known as a temple where all nations may pray.”
7.note: tn Heb “for my house will be called a house of prayer for all the nations.”

8.note: tn The meaning of the statement is unclear. The text reads literally, “Still I will gather upon him to his gathered ones.” Perhaps the preposition -לְ (lamed) before “gathered ones” introduces the object of the verb, as in Jer 49:5. The third masculine singular suffix on both עָלָיו (’alayv) and נִקְבָּצָיו (niqbatsayv) probably refers to “Israel.” In this case one can translate literally, “Still I will gather to him his gathered ones.”

10.anchor: They pant,
10.note: tn The Hebrew text has הֹזִים (hozim), which appears to be derived from an otherwise unattested verbal root הָזָה (hazah). On the basis of alleged cognates, BDB 223 s.v. הָזָה offers the definition “dream, rave” while HALOT 243 s.v. הזה lists “pant.” In this case the dog metaphor of the preceding lines continues. The reference to dogs at the beginning of v. 11 favors the extension of the metaphor. The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa has חזים (“seers”) here. In this case the “watchmen” are directly identified as prophets and depicted as lazy.

11.anchor: each one looking for monetary gain.
11.note: tn Heb “for his gain from his end.”

12.anchor: We’ll have everything we want!’
12.note: tn Heb “great, [in] abundance, very much,” i.e., “very great indeed.” See HALOT 452 s.v. יֶתֶר.

1.note: tc The form in the Hebrew text appears to derive from an otherwise unattested verb נָלָה (nalah). The translation follows the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa in reading ככלתך, a Piel infinitival form from the verbal root כָּלָה (kalah), meaning “finish.”

2.note: tn Heb “[Be] also our deliverance in the time of distress.”

3.anchor: the nations scatter when you spring into action!
3.note: tn Heb “because of your exaltation the nations scatter.”

4.anchor: they swarm over it like locusts!
4.note: tn Heb “like a swarm of locusts swarming on it.”

5.anchor: indeed,
5.note: tn Or “for” (KJV, NASB, NIV).

5.anchor: he lives in heaven;
5.note: tn Heb “on high” (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV); CEV “in the heavens.”

6.anchor: he gives all this to those who fear him.
6.note: tn Heb “the fear of the Lord, it is his treasure.”

7.anchor: messengers sent to make peace
7.note: tn Heb “messengers of peace,” apparently those responsible for negotiating the agreements that have been broken (see v. 8).

8.anchor: human life is treated with disrespect.
8.note: tn Heb “he does not regard human beings.”

9.note: sn Both of these areas were known for their trees and vegetation. See 2:13; 35:2.
tn Heb “shake off [their leaves]” (so ASV, NRSV); NAB “are stripped bare.”

10.anchor: now I will magnify myself.
10.note: tn Or “lift myself up” (KJV); NLT “show my power and might.”

11.anchor: your breath is a fire that destroys you.
11.note: sn The hostile nations’ plans to destroy God’s people will come to nothing; their hostility will end up being self-destructive.

12.anchor: The nations will be burned to ashes;
12.note: tn Heb “will be a burning to lime.” See Amos 2:1.

14.anchor: Who among us can coexist with unquenchable
14.note: tn Or “perpetual”; or “everlasting” (KJV, ASV, NAB, NIV, NRSV).

15.anchor: and does not seek to harm others
15.note: tn Heb “[who] closes his eyes from seeing evil.”

16.anchor: he will find safety in the rocky, mountain strongholds;
16.note: tn Heb “mountain strongholds, cliffs [will be] his elevated place.”

17.anchor: you will see a wide land.
17.note: tn Heb “a land of distances,” i.e., an extensive land.

18.anchor: Where is the one who counts the towers?”
18.note: sn The people refer to various Assyrian officials who were responsible for determining the amount of taxation or tribute Judah must pay to the Assyrian king.

19.anchor: whose derisive speech you do not understand.
19.note: tn Heb “derision of tongue there is no understanding.” The Niphal of לָעַג (la’ag) occurs only here. In the Qal and Hiphil the verb means “to deride, mock.” A related noun is used in 28:11.

20.anchor: a tent that stays put;
20.note: tn Or “that does not travel”; NASB “which shall not be folded.”

21.anchor: no large ships will sail through.
21.note: tn Heb “and a mighty ship will not pass through it.”

23.anchor: even the lame will drag off plunder.
23.note: sn Judah’s victory over its enemies will be so thorough there will be more than enough plunder for everyone, even slow-moving lame men who would normally get left out in the rush to gather the loot.

24.anchor: No resident of Zion
24.note: tn The words “of Zion” are supplied in the translation for clarification.

1.anchor: confront Jacob’s family with their sin!
1.note: tn Heb “and to the house of Jacob their sin.” The verb “declare” is understood by ellipsis (note the preceding line).

2.anchor: they want to know my requirements,
2.note: tn Heb “ways” (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV, TEV); NLT “my laws.”

3.anchor: you oppress your workers.
3.note: tn Or perhaps, “debtors.” See HALOT 865 s.v. * עָצֵב.

4.anchor: and fistfights.
4.note: tn Heb “and for striking with a sinful fist.”

5.anchor: and stretching out
5.note: tn Or “making [their] bed.”

6.note: tn Heb “crushed.”

7.anchor: Don’t turn your back on your own flesh and blood!
7.note: tn Heb “and from your flesh do not hide yourself.”

8.anchor: ’s splendor will be your rear guard.
8.note: sn The nation will experience God’s protective presence.

9.note: tn Heb “if you.” In the Hebrew text vv. 9b-10 are one long conditional sentence. The protasis (“if” clauses appear in vv. 9b-10a), with the apodosis (“then” clause) appearing in v. 10b.

10.anchor: and your darkness will be transformed into noonday.
10.note: tn Heb “and your darkness [will be] like noonday.”

11.anchor: He will give you renewed strength,
11.note: tn Heb “and your bones he will strengthen.”

12.anchor: the one who makes the streets inhabitable again.’
12.note: tc The Hebrew text has “the one who restores paths for dwelling.” The idea of “paths to dwell in” is not a common notion. Some have proposed emending נְתִיבוֹת (nÿtivot, “paths”) to נְתִיצוֹת (nÿtitsot, “ruins”), a passive participle from נָתַץ (natats, “tear down”; see HALOT 732 s.v. *נְתִיצָה), because tighter parallelism with the preceding line is achieved. However, none of the textual sources support this emendation. The line may mean that paths must be repaired in order to dwell in the land.

13.anchor: and by refraining from your selfish pursuits and from making business deals.
13.note: tn Heb “and you honor it [by refraining] from accomplishing your ways, from finding your desire and speaking a word.” It is unlikely that the last phrase (“speaking a word”) is a prohibition against talking on the Sabbath; instead it probably refers to making transactions or plans (see Hos 10:4). Some see here a reference to idle talk (cf. 2 Sam 19:30).

14.note: tn Heb “for the mouth of the Lord has spoken.” The introductory כִּי (ki) may be asseverative (as reflected in the translation) or causal/explanatory, explaining why the preceding promise will become reality (because it is guaranteed by the divine word).

2.note: sn Jeshurun is a poetic name for Israel; it occurs here and in Deut 32:15; 33:5, 26.

3.anchor: and cause streams to flow
3.note: tn Heb “and streams”; KJV “floods.” The verb “cause…to flow” is supplied in the second line for clarity and for stylistic reasons.

4.anchor: They will sprout up like a tree in the grass,
4.note: tn The Hebrew term בֵין (ven) is usually taken as a preposition, in which case one might translate, “among the grass.” But בֵין is probably the name of a tree (cf. C. R. North, Second Isaiah, 133). If one alters the preposition bet (בְּ) to kaf (כְּ), one can then read, “like a binu-tree.” (The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa supports this reading.) This forms a nice parallel to “like poplars” in the next line. חָצִיר (khatsir) is functioning as an adverbial accusative of location.

5.anchor: and use the name ‘Israel.’”
5.note: tn Heb “and by the name of Israel he will title.” Some, with support from several ancient versions, prefer to change the Piel (active) verb form to a Pual (passive), “and he will be titled by the name of Israel.”

6.note: tn Heb “his kinsman redeemer.” See the note at 41:14.

7.anchor: let them announce future events!
7.note: tn Heb and those things which are coming let them declare for themselves.”

8.anchor: There is no other sheltering rock;
8.note: tn Heb “rock” or “rocky cliff,” a title that depicts God as a protective refuge in his role as sovereign king; thus the translation “sheltering rock.”

10.anchor: that will prove worthless?
10.note: tn The rhetorical question is sarcastic. The sense is, “Who is foolish enough…?”

11.anchor: the craftsmen are mere humans.
11.note: sn The point seems to be this: If the idols are the mere products of human hands, then those who trust in them will be disappointed, for man-made gods are incapable of helping their “creators.”

12.anchor: He gets hungry and loses his energy;
12.note: tn Heb “and there is no strength”; NASB “his strength fails.”

13.anchor: and puts it in a shrine.
13.note: tn Heb “like the glory of man to sit [in] a house”; NIV “that it may dwell in a shrine.”

14.note: tn Some prefer to emend אֹרֶן (’oren) to אֶרֶז (’erez, “cedar”), but the otherwise unattested noun appears to have an Akkadian cognate, meaning “cedar.” See H. R. Cohen, Biblical Hapax Legomena (SBLDS), 44-45. HALOT 90 s.v. I אֹרֶן offers the meaning “laurel.”

15.anchor: he makes an idol and bows down to it.
15.note: tn Or perhaps, “them.”

16.anchor: over that half he cooks
16.note: tn Heb “eats” (so NASB); NAB, NRSV “roasts.”

18.anchor: their minds do not discern.
18.note: tn Heb “for their eyes are smeared over so they cannot see, so their heart cannot be wise.”

19.anchor: Should I bow down to dry wood?’
19.note: tn There is no formal interrogative sign here, but the context seems to indicate these are rhetorical questions. See GKC 473 §150.a.

20.anchor: nor does he say, ‘Is this not a false god I hold in my right hand?’
20.note: tn Heb “Is it not a lie in my right hand?”

21.anchor: O Israel, I will not forget you!
21.note: tc The verb in the Hebrew text is a Niphal imperfect with a pronominal suffix. Although the Niphal ordinarily has the passive sense, it can have a reflexive nuance as well (see above translation). Some have suggested an emendation to a Qal form: “Do not forget me” (all the ancient versions, NEB, REB; see GKC 369 §117.x). “Do not forget me” would make a good parallel with “remember these things” in the first line. Since the MT is the harder reading and fits with Israel’s complaint that God had forgotten her (Isa 40:27), the MT reading should be retained (NASB, NKJV, NRSV, ESV). The passive has been rendered as an active in the translation in keeping with contemporary English style (so also NIV, NCV, TEV, NLT).

22.anchor: Come back to me, for I protect
22.note: tn Heb “redeem.” See the note at 41:14.

23.anchor: he reveals his splendor through Israel.
23.note: tn That is, by delivering Israel. Cf. NCV “showed his glory when he saved Israel”; TEV “has shown his greatness by saving his people Israel.”

24.anchor: who fashioned the earth all by myself,
24.note: tn The consonantal text (Kethib) has “Who [was] with me?” The marginal reading (Qere) is “from with me,” i.e., “by myself.” See BDB 87 s.v. II אֵת 4.c.

25.anchor: and makes their advice
25.note: tn Heb “their knowledge” (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NRSV).

26.note: map For location see Map5-B1; Map6-F3; Map7-E2; Map8-F2; Map10-B3; JP1-F4; JP2-F4; JP3-F4; JP4-F4.

28.anchor: and concerning the temple, ‘It will be reconstructed.’”
28.note: tn Heb “and [concerning the] temple, you will be founded.” The preposition -לְ (lÿ) is understood by ellipsis at the beginning of the second line. The verb תִּוָּסֵד (tivvased, “you will be founded”) is second masculine singular and is probably addressed to the personified temple (הֵיכָל [hekhal, “temple”] is masculine).

1.anchor: his ear is not too deaf to hear you.
1.note: tn Heb “or his ear too heavy [i.e., “dull”] to hear.”

2.anchor: your sins have caused him to reject you and not listen to your prayers.
2.note: tn Heb “and your sins have caused [his] face to be hidden from you so as not to hear.”

4.anchor: they conceive of oppression
4.note: tn Or “trouble” (NIV), or “harm.”

5.anchor: a poisonous snake is hatched.
5.note: tn Heb “that which is pressed in hatches [as] a snake.”

6.note: tn Heb “their deeds are deeds of sin, and the work of violence [is] in their hands.”

7.anchor: they crush and destroy.
7.note: tn Heb “their thoughts are thoughts of sin, destruction and crushing [are] in their roadways.”

8.anchor: and whoever deals with them is unfamiliar with peace.
8.note: tn Heb “their paths they make crooked, everyone who walks in it does not know peace.”

9.note: tn The words “we wait for” are supplied in the translation; the verb is understood by ellipsis (note the preceding line).
tn The plural noun form may indicate degree here.
tn Or “walk about”; NCV “all we have is darkness.”
tn The plural noun form may indicate degree here.

10.anchor: Though others are strong, we are like dead men.
10.note: tn Heb among the strong, like dead men.”

11.anchor: we wait for deliverance,
11.note: tn See the note at v. 9.

12.anchor: we know our sins all too well.
12.note: tn Heb “indeed [or “for”] our rebellious deeds (are) with us, and our sins, we know them.”

13.anchor: we tell lies we concocted in our minds.
13.note: tn Heb “conceiving and uttering from the heart words of falsehood.”

14.note: tn Or “for” (KJV, NRSV).

15.anchor: watches and is displeased,
15.note: tn Heb “and it is displeasing in his eyes.”

16.anchor: his desire for justice drives him on.
16.note: tn Heb “and his justice [or “righteousness”] supports him.”

17.anchor: He puts on the garments of vengeance
17.note: tn Heb “and he puts on the clothes of vengeance [as] a garment.”

18.anchor: He repays the coastlands.
18.note: tn Or “islands” (KJV, NIV).

19.note: tn Heb “the wind of the Lord drives it on.” The term רוּחַ (ruakh) could be translated “breath” here (see 30:28).

20.anchor: to those in Jacob who repent of their rebellious deeds,”
20.note: tn Heb “and to those who turn from rebellion in Jacob.”

21.anchor: “As for me, this is my promise to
21.note: tn Or “my covenant with” (so many English versions); NCV “my agreement with.”sn The Lord promises the repentant (note “to them”) that they and their offspring will possess his spirit and function as his spokesmen. In this regard they follow in the footsteps of the Lord’s special servant. See 42:1; 49:2; 51:16.

21.anchor: . “My spirit, who is upon you, and my words, which I have placed in your mouth, will not depart from your mouth or from the mouths of your children and descendants from this time forward,”
21.note: tn Heb “from now and on into the future.”

1.anchor: and who is able to deliver!”
1.note: tn Heb “I, [the one] speaking in vindication [or “righteousness”], great to deliver.”

2.anchor: Why do you look like someone who has stomped on grapes in a vat?
2.note: tn Heb “and your garments like one who treads in a vat?”

3.anchor: and stained
3.note: tn Heb “and I stained.” For discussion of the difficult verb form, see HALOT 170 s.v. II גאל. Perhaps the form is mixed, combining the first person forms of the imperfect (note the alef prefix) and perfect (note the תי- ending).

4.anchor: and then payback time arrived.
4.note: tn Heb “for the day of vengeance was in my heart, and the year of my revenge came.” The term גְּאוּלַי (gÿ’ulai) is sometimes translated here “my redemption,” for the verbal root גאל often means “deliver, buy back.” A גֹּאֵל (go’el, “kinsman-redeemer”) was responsible for protecting the extended family’s interests, often by redeeming property that had been sold outside the family. However, the responsibilities of a גֹּאֵל extended beyond financial concerns. He was also responsible for avenging the shed blood of a family member (see Num 35:19-27; Deut 19:6-12). In Isa 63:4, where vengeance is a prominent theme (note the previous line), it is probably this function of the family protector that is in view. The Lord pictures himself as a blood avenger who waits for the day of vengeance to arrive and then springs into action.

5.anchor: my raging anger drove me on.
5.note: tn Heb “and my anger, it supported me”; NIV “my own wrath sustained me.”

6.anchor: I splashed their blood on the ground.”
6.note: tn Heb “and I brought down to the ground their juice.” “Juice” refers to their blood (see v. 3).

7.anchor: because of
7.note: tn Heb “according to.”

8.note: tn Heb “children [who] do not act deceitfully.” Here the verb refers to covenantal loyalty.

9.anchor: he lifted them up and carried them throughout ancient times.
9.note: tn Heb “all the days of antiquity”; KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV “days of old.”

10.anchor: But they rebelled and offended
10.note: tn Or “grieved, hurt the feelings of.”

10.anchor: his holy Spirit,
10.note: sn The phrase “holy Spirit” occurs in the OT only here (in v. 11 as well) and in Ps 51:11 (51:13 HT), where it is associated with the divine presence.

11.anchor: Where is the one who placed his holy Spirit among them,
11.note: sn See the note at v. 10.

12.anchor: gaining for himself a lasting reputation,
12.note: tn Heb “making for himself a lasting name.”

13.anchor: Like a horse running on flat land
13.note: tn Heb “in the desert [or “steppe”].”

14.anchor: gaining for yourself an honored reputation.
14.note: tn Heb “making for yourself a majestic name.”

15.anchor: Do not hold back your tender compassion!
15.note: tn The Hebrew text reads literally, “the agitation of your intestines and your compassion to me they are held back.” The phrase “agitation of your intestines” is metonymic, referring to the way in which one’s nervous system reacts when one feels pity and compassion toward another. אֵלַי (’elay, “to me”) is awkward in this context, where the speaker represents the nation and, following the introduction (see v. 7), utilizes first person plural forms. The translation assumes an emendation to the negative particle אַל (’al). This also necessitates emending the following verb form (which is a plural perfect) to a singular jussive (תִתְאַפָּק, tit’appaq). The Hitpael of אָפַק (’afaq) also occurs in 42:14.

16.anchor: you have been called our protector from ancient times.
16.note: tn Heb “our protector [or “redeemer”] from antiquity [is] your name.”

17.anchor: and make our minds stubborn so that we do not obey you?
17.note: tn Heb “[Why do] you harden our heart[s] so as not to fear you.” The interrogative particle is understood by ellipsis (note the preceding line).sn How direct this hardening is, one cannot be sure. The speaker may envision direct involvement on the Lord’s part. The Lord has brought the exile as judgment for the nation’s sin and now he continues to keep them at arm’s length by blinding them spiritually. The second half of 64:7 might support this, though the precise reading of the final verb is uncertain. On the other hand, the idiom of lament is sometimes ironic and hyperbolically deterministic. For example, Naomi lamented that Shaddai was directly opposing her and bringing her calamity (Ruth 1:20-21), while the author of Ps 88 directly attributes his horrible suffering and loneliness to God (see especially vv. 6-8, 16-18). Both individuals make little, if any, room for intermediate causes or the principle of sin and death which ravages the human race. In the same way, the speaker in Isa 63:17 (who evidences great spiritual sensitivity and is anything but “hardened”) may be referring to the hardships of exile, which discouraged and even embittered the people, causing many of them to retreat from their Yahwistic faith. In this case, the “hardening” in view is more indirect and can be lifted by the Lord’s intervention. Whether the hardening here is indirect or direct, it is important to recognize that the speaker sees it as one of the effects of rebellion against the Lord (note especially 64:5-6).

18.anchor: but then our adversaries knocked down
18.note: tn Heb “your adversaries trampled on.”

19.anchor: they were not your subjects.
19.note: tn Heb “you did not rule them, your name was not called over them.” The expression “the name is called over” indicates ownership; see the note at 4:1. As these two lines stand they are very difficult to interpret. They appear to be stating that the adversaries just mentioned in v. 18 have not been subject to the Lord’s rule in the past, perhaps explaining why they could commit the atrocity described in v. 18b.

1.note: sn The statement is an oxymoron. Its ironic quality adds to its rhetorical impact. The statement reminds one of the norm (one must normally buy commodities) as it expresses the astounding offer. One might paraphrase the statement: “Come and take freely what you normally have to pay for.”

2.note: tn Heb “Let your appetite delight in fine food.”sn Nourishing, fine food here represents the blessings God freely offers. These include forgiveness, a new covenantal relationship with God, and national prominence (see vv. 3-6).

3.anchor: just like the reliable covenantal promises I made to David.
3.note: tn Heb “the reliable expressions of loyalty of David.” The syntactical relationship of חַסְדֵי (khasde, “expressions of loyalty”) to the preceding line is unclear. If the term is appositional to בְּרִית (bÿrit, “covenant”), then the Lord here transfers the promises of the Davidic covenant to the entire nation. Another option is to take חַסְדֵי (khasde) as an adverbial accusative and to translate “according to the reliable covenantal promises.” In this case the new covenantal arrangement proposed here is viewed as an extension or perhaps fulfillment of the Davidic promises. A third option, the one reflected in the above translation, is to take the last line as comparative. In this case the new covenant being proposed is analogous to the Davidic covenant. Verses 4-5, which compare David’s international prominence to what Israel will experience, favors this view. In all three of these interpretations, “David” is an objective genitive; he is the recipient of covenantal promises. A fourth option would be to take David as a subjective genitive and understand the line as giving the basis for the preceding promise: “Then I will make an unconditional covenantal promise to you, because of David’s faithful acts of covenantal loyalty.”

4.anchor: Look, I made him a witness to nations,
4.note: sn Ideally the Davidic king was to testify to the nations of God’s greatness (cf. Pss 18:50 HT [18:49 ET]; 22:28 HT [22:27 ET]). See J. H. Eaton, Kingship in the Psalms (SBT), 182-84.

5.anchor: the Holy One of Israel,
5.note: sn See the note on the phrase “the Holy One of Israel” in 1:4.

6.note: tn Heb “while he allows himself to be found.” The Niphal form has a tolerative force here.

7.anchor: and to their God, for he will freely forgive them.
7.note: sn The appeal and promise of vv. 6-7 echoes the language of Deut 4:25-31; 30:1-10; and 1 Kgs 8:46-53, all of which anticipate the exile and speak of the prerequisites for restoration.

8.note: tn Heb “ways” (so many English versions).
tn Heb “are not.” “Like” is interpretive, but v. 9 indicates that a comparison is in view.

9.note: tn Or “thoughts” (so many English versions).

10.note: tn This verse begins in the Hebrew text with כִּי כַּאֲשֶׁר (ki ka’asher, “for, just as”), which is completed by כֵּן (ken, “so, in the same way”) at the beginning of v. 11. For stylistic reasons, this lengthy sentence is divided up into separate sentences in the translation.

11.anchor: and is fulfilled as I intend.”
11.note: tn Heb “but it accomplishes what I desire, and succeeds [on the mission] which I send it.”sn Verses 8-11 focus on the reliability of the divine word and support the promises before (vv. 3-5, 7b) and after (vv. 12-13) this. Israel can be certain that repentance will bring forgiveness and a new covenantal relationship because God’s promises are reliable. In contrast to human plans (or “thoughts”), which are destined to fail (Ps 94:11) apart from divine approval (Prov 19:21), and human deeds (or “ways”), which are evil and lead to destruction (Prov 1:15-19; 3:31-33; 4:19), God’s plans are realized and his deeds accomplish something positive.

13.anchor: a permanent reminder that will remain.
13.note: tn Or, more literally, “a permanent sign that will not be cut off.”

1.note: tn Or “glory” (so most English versions).

2.note: tn Or “glory” (so most English versions); TEV “the brightness of his presence.”

4.anchor: Look all around you!
4.note: tn Heb “Lift up around your eyes and see!”

5.anchor: For the riches of distant lands
5.note: tn Heb “the wealth of the sea,” i.e., wealth that is transported from distant lands via the sea.

6.note: tn Heb “and they will announce the praises of the Lord.”

7.anchor: They will go up on my altar acceptably,
7.note: tc Heb “they will go up on acceptance [on] my altar.” Some have suggested that the preposition עַל (’al) is dittographic (note the preceding יַעֲלוּ [ya’alu]). Consequently, the form should be emended to לְרָצוֹן (lÿratson, “acceptably”; see BDB 953 s.v. רָצוֹן). However, the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa has both לרצון followed by the preposition על, which would argue against deleted the preposition. As the above translation seeks to demonstrate, the preposition עַל (’al) indicates a norm (“in accordance with acceptance” or “acceptably”; IBHS 218 §11.2.13e, n. 111) and the “altar” functions as an objective accusative with a verb of motion (cf. Gen 49:4; Lev 2:2; Num 13:17; J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah [NICOT], 2:534, n. 14).

8.anchor: who fly like doves to their shelters?
8.note: tn Heb “to their windows,” i.e., to the openings in their coops. See HALOT 83 s.v. אֲרֻבָּה.

9.note: sn See the note on the phrase “the Holy One of Israel” in 1:4.

10.anchor: I will restore my favor and have compassion on you.
10.note: tn Heb “in my favor I will have compassion on you.”

11.anchor: with their kings leading the way.
11.note: tn Or “led in procession.” The participle is passive.

12.anchor: such nations will be totally destroyed.
12.note: tn The infinitive absolute appears before the finite verb for emphasis.

13.anchor: I will bestow honor on my throne room.
13.note: tn Heb “the place of my feet.” See Ezek 43:7, where the Lord’s throne is called the “place of the soles of my feet.”

14.anchor: Zion of the Holy One of Israel.’
14.note: sn See the note on the phrase “the Holy One of Israel” in 1:4.

15.anchor: but I will make you
15.note: tn Heb “Instead of your being abandoned and despised, with no one passing through, I will make you.”

16.anchor: your protector,
16.note: tn Or “redeemer.” See the note at 41:14.

16.anchor: the powerful ruler of Jacob.
16.note: sn See 1:24 and 49:26.

17.anchor: and vindication your sovereign ruler.
17.note: tn The plural indicates degree. The language is ironic; in the past Zion was ruled by oppressive tyrants, but now personified prosperity and vindication will be the only things that will “dominate” the city.

18.anchor: or the sounds of
18.note: tn The words “sounds of” are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.

19.anchor: the splendor of your God will shine upon you.
19.note: tn Heb “and your God for your splendor.”

20.anchor: your time
20.note: tn Heb “days” (so KJV, NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT).

21.anchor: through whom I reveal my splendor.
21.note: tn Heb “a shoot of his planting, the work of my hands, to reveal splendor.”

22.anchor: will quickly do this!”
22.note: tn Heb “I, the Lord, in its time, I will quickly do it.”

1.note: tn Or “quake.” נָזֹלּוּ (nazollu) is from the verbal root זָלַל (zalal, “quake”; see HALOT 272 s.v. II זלל). Perhaps there is a verbal allusion to Judg 5:5, the only other passage where this verb occurs. In that passage the poet tells how the Lord’s appearance to do battle caused the mountains to shake.

2.anchor: let your adversaries know who you are,
2.note: tn Heb “to make known your name to your adversaries.” Perhaps the infinitive construct with preposition -לְ (lamed) should be construed with “come down” in v. 1a, or subordinated to the following line: “To make known your name to your adversaries, let the nations shake from before you.”

3.anchor: you came down, and the mountains trembled
3.note: tn See the note at v. 1.

4.anchor: Since ancient times no one has heard or perceived,
4.note: tn Heb “from ancient times they have not heard, they have not listened.”

5.anchor: How then can we be saved?
5.note: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, “look, you were angry and we sinned against them continually [or perhaps, “in ancient times”] and we were delivered.” The statement makes little sense as it stands. The first vav [ו] consecutive (“and we sinned”) must introduce an explanatory clause here (see Num 1:48 and Isa 39:1 for other examples of this relatively rare use of the vav [ו] consecutive). The final verb (if rendered positively) makes no sense in this context – God’s anger at their sin resulted in judgment, not deliverance. One of the alternatives involves an emendation to וַנִּרְשָׁע (vannirsha’, “and we were evil”; LXX, NRSV, TEV). The Vulgate and the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa support the MT reading. One can either accept an emendation or cast the statement as a question (as above).

6.anchor: all our so-called righteous acts are like a menstrual rag in your sight.
6.note: tn Heb “and like a garment of menstruation [are] all our righteous acts”; KJV, NIV “filthy rags”; ASV “a polluted garment.”

7.anchor: and handed us over to our own sins.
7.note: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, “and you caused us to melt in the hand of our sin.” The verb וַתְּמוּגֵנוּ (vattÿmugenu) is a Qal preterite 2nd person masculine singular with a 1st person common plural suffix from the root מוּג (mug, “melt”). However, elsewhere the Qal of this verb is intransitive. If the verbal root מוּג (mug) is retained here, the form should be emended to a Polel pattern (וַתְּמֹגְגֵנוּ, vattÿmogÿgenu). The translation assumes an emendation to וַתְּמַגְּנֵנוּ (vattÿmaggÿnenu, “and you handed us over”). This form is a Piel preterite 2nd person masculine singular with a 1st person common plural suffix from the verbal root מִגֵּן (miggen, “hand over, surrender”; see HALOT 545 s.v. מגן and BDB 171 s.v. מָגָן). The point is that God has abandoned them to their sinful ways and no longer seeks reconciliation.

8.anchor: we are all the product of your labor.
8.note: tn Heb “the work of your hand.”

9.anchor: Take a good look at your people, at all of us!
9.note: tn Heb “Look, gaze at your people, all of us.” Another option is to translate, “Take a good look! We are all your people.”

10.anchor: Jerusalem
10.note: map For location see Map5-B1; Map6-F3; Map7-E2; Map8-F2; Map10-B3; JP1-F4; JP2-F4; JP3-F4; JP4-F4.

11.anchor: all our prized possessions have been destroyed.
11.note: tn Or “all that we valued has become a ruin.”

12.anchor: In light of all this,
12.note: tn Heb “because of these”; KJV, ASV “for these things.”

1.anchor: to a nation that did not invoke
1.note: tn Heb “call out in”; NASB, NIV, NRSV “call on.”

2.anchor: and who did what they desired.
2.note: tn Heb “who walked [in] the way that is not good, after their thoughts.”

3.anchor: and burn incense on brick altars.
3.note: tn Or perhaps, “on tiles.”

4.anchor: and broth
4.note: tc The marginal reading (Qere), supported by the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa, reads מְרַק (mÿraq, “broth”), while the consonantal text (Kethib) has פְרַק (feraq, “fragment”).

6.anchor: I will pay them back exactly what they deserve,
6.note: tn Heb “I will pay back into their lap.”

7.anchor: I will punish them in full measure.”
7.note: tn Heb “I will measure out their pay [from the] beginning into their lap,” i.e., he will give them everything they have earned.

8.note: tn Heb “by not destroying everyone.”

9.anchor: My chosen ones will take possession of the land;
9.note: tn Heb “it.” The third feminine singular pronominal suffix probably refers to the land which contains the aforementioned mountains.

10.anchor: they will belong to my people, who seek me.
10.note: tn Heb “for my people who seek me.”

11.anchor: and fill up wine jugs for the god called ‘Destiny’
11.note: tn The Hebrew has לַמְנִי (lamni, “for Meni”), the name of a pagan deity. See HALOT 602 s.v. מְגִי.

12.anchor: You did evil before me;
12.note: tn Heb “that which is evil in my eyes.”

14.anchor: you will wail because your spirits will be crushed.
14.note: tn Heb “from the breaking of the spirit.”

15.anchor: Your names will live on in the curse formulas of my chosen ones.
15.note: tn Heb “you will leave your name for an oath to my chosen ones.”sn For an example of such a curse formula see Jer 29:22.

16.anchor: I will no longer think about them.
16.note: tn Heb “for the former distresses will be forgotten, and they will be hidden from my eyes.”

17.anchor: no one will think about them anymore.
17.note: tn Heb “and they will not come up on the mind.”

18.anchor: and her people to be a source of happiness.
18.note: tn Heb “her people, happiness.” See the preceding note.

19.anchor: and my people will bring me happiness.
19.note: tn Heb “and I will rejoice in Jerusalem, and be happy in my people.”

20.anchor: anyone who fails to reach
20.note: tn Heb “the one who misses.” חָטָא (khata’) is used here in its basic sense of “miss the mark.” See HALOT 305 s.v. חטא. Another option is to translate, “and the sinner who reaches the age of a hundred will be cursed.”

22.anchor: and my chosen ones will enjoy to the fullest what they have produced.
22.note: tn Heb “the work of their hands” (so KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV); NLT “their hard-won gains.”

23.anchor: and their descendants.
23.note: tn Heb “for offspring blessed by the Lord they [will be], and their descendants along with them.”

24.anchor: Before they even call out,
24.note: tn The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on “in the future” in 2:2.

25.anchor: on my entire royal mountain,”
25.note: tn Heb “in all my holy mountain.” These same words appear in 11:9. See the note there.sn As in 11:1-9 the prophet anticipates a time when the categories predator-prey no longer exist. See the note at the end of 11:8.

1.note: tn Heb “goes forth like brightness.”

2.note: tn Heb “which the mouth of the Lord will designate.”

4.anchor: and your land will be married to him.
4.note: tn That is, the land will be restored to the Lord’s favor and once again enjoy his blessing and protection. To indicate the land’s relationship to the Lord, the words “to him” have been supplied at the end of the clause.

5.anchor: so your sons
5.note: tc The Hebrew text has “your sons,” but this produces an odd metaphor and is somewhat incongruous with the parallelism. In the context (v. 4b, see also 54:5-7) the Lord is the one who “marries” Zion. Therefore several prefer to emend “your sons” to בֹּנָיִךְ (bonayikh, “your builder”; e.g., NRSV). In Ps 147:2 the Lord is called the “builder of Jerusalem.” However, this emendation is not the best option for at least four reasons. First, although the Lord is never called the “builder” of Jerusalem in Isaiah, the idea of Zion’s children possessing the land does occur (Isa 49:20; 54:3; cf. also 14:1; 60:21). Secondly, all the ancient versions support the MT reading. Thirdly, although the verb בָּעַל (ba’al) can mean “to marry,” its basic idea is “to possess.” Consequently, the verb stresses a relationship more than a state. All the ancient versions render this verb “to dwell in” or “to dwell with.” The point is not just that the land will be reinhabited, but that it will be in a relationship of “belonging” to the Israelites. Hence a relational verb like בָּעַל is used (J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah [NICOT], 2:581). Finally, “sons” is a well-known metaphor for “inhabitants” (J. de Waard, Isaiah, 208).

6.note: tn Or “invoke”; NIV “call on”; NASB, NRSV “remind.”

7.anchor: until he makes Jerusalem the pride
7.note: tn Heb “[the object of] praise.”

8.note: tn The Lord’s right hand and strong arm here symbolize his power and remind the audience that his might guarantees the fulfillment of the following promise.

9.anchor: Those who pick the grapes will drink the wine
9.note: tn Heb “and those who gather it will drink it.” The masculine singular pronominal suffixes attached to “gather” and “drink” refer back to the masculine noun תִּירוֹשׁ (tirosh, “wine”) in v. 8b.

11.anchor: and his reward goes before him!’”
11.note: sn As v. 12 indicates, the returning exiles are the Lord’s reward/prize. See also 40:10 and the note there.

12.anchor: the Ones Protected
12.note: tn Or “the redeemed of the Lord” (KJV, NAB).

1.note: tn Heb “to bind up [the wounds of].”

2.anchor: the day when our God will seek vengeance,
