2.note: tn Heb “to announce the year of the Lord’s favor, and the day of our God’s vengeance.

3.anchor: to reveal his splendor.
3.note: tn Heb “a planting of the Lord to reveal splendor.”

4.anchor: and restore the places that were desolate;
4.note: tn Heb “and the formerly desolate places they will raise up.”

5.note: sn The Lord speaks in vv. 7-8 (and possibly v. 9). It is not clear where the servant’s speech (see vv. 1-3a) ends and the Lord’s begins. Perhaps the direct address to the people signals the beginning of the Lord’s speech.

5.anchor: “Foreigners will take care of
5.note: tn Heb “will stand [in position] and shepherd.”

6.note: tc The form in the Hebrew text is probably a corruption of יִתְאַמְּרוּ (yit’ammÿru), a Hitpael from אָמַר (’amar), meaning “boast about” (see HALOT 67 s.v. II אמר, HALOT 416 s.v. ימר, and BDB 56 s.v. אָמַר).

6.anchor: the riches you receive from them.
6.note: tn Heb “their glory” (i.e., riches).

7.anchor: Yes,
7.note: tn Heb “therefore” (so KJV, NASB); NIV “and so.”

8.anchor: I will repay them because of my faithfulness;
8.note: tn Heb “in faithfulness”; NASB, NRSV, NLT “faithfully.”

9.note: tn Heb “all who see them will recognize them, that they [are] descendants [whom] the Lord has blessed.”

10.anchor: I look like a bride when she puts on her jewelry.
10.note: tn Heb “like a bridegroom [who] acts like a priest [by wearing] a turban, and like a bride [who] wears her jewelry.” The words “I look” are supplied for stylistic reasons and clarification.

11.anchor: and give his people reason to praise him in the sight of all the nations.
11.note: tn Heb “and praise before all the nations.”

1.note: tn Or “righteous” (KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT); NAB “the just man.”
tn Heb “are taken away.” The Niphal of אָסַף (’asaf) here means “to die.”

tn The term מִפְּנֵי (mippÿne, “from the face of”) often has a causal nuance. It also appears with the Niphal of אָסַף (’asaph, “gather”) in 2 Chr 12:5: אֲשֶׁר־נֶאֶסְפוּ אֶל־יְרוּשָׁלַם מִפְּנֵי שִׁישָׁק (’asher-ne’esphu ’el-yÿrushalam mippÿney shishaq, “who had gathered at Jerusalem because of [i.e., due to fear of] Shishak”).

tn The translation assumes that this verse, in proverbial fashion, laments society’s apathy over the persecution of the godly. The second half of the verse observes that such apathy results in more widespread oppression. Since the next verse pictures the godly being taken to a place of rest, some interpret the second half of v. 1 in a more positive vein. According to proponents of this view, God removes the godly so that they might be spared suffering and calamity, a fact which the general populace fails to realize.

2.note: tn Heb “he enters peace, they rest on their beds, the one who walks straight ahead of himself.” The tomb is here viewed in a fairly positive way as a place where the dead are at peace and sleep undisturbed.

3.anchor: you offspring of adulteresses and prostitutes!
3.note: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, “offspring of an adulterer [masculine] and [one who] has committed adultery.” Perhaps the text has suffered from transposition of vav (ו) and tav (ת) and מְנָאֵף וַתִּזְנֶה (mÿna’ef vattizneh) should be emended to מְנָאֶפֶת וְזֹנָה (mÿna’efet vÿzonah, “an adulteress and a prostitute”). Both singular nouns would be understood in a collective sense. Most modern English versions render both forms as nouns.

4.anchor: the offspring of liars,
4.note: tn Heb “Are you not children of rebellion, offspring of a lie?” The rhetorical question anticipates the answer, “Of course you are!”

5.anchor: who slaughter children near the streams under the rocky overhangs.
5.note: sn This apparently alludes to the practice of child sacrifice (cf. TEV, CEV, NLT).

6.anchor: Because of these things I will seek vengeance.
6.note: tn The text reads literally, “Because of these am I relenting?” If the prefixed interrogative particle is retained at the beginning of the sentence, then the question would be rhetorical, with the Niphal of נָחָם (nakham) probably being used in the sense of “relent, change one’s mind.” One could translate: “Because of these things, how can I relent?” However, the initial letter he may be dittographic (note the final he [ה] on the preceding word). In this case one may understand the verb in the sense of “console oneself, seek vengeance,” as in 1:24.

8.note: tn The Hebrew text has simply חָזָה (khazah, “gaze”). The adverb “longingly” is interpretive (see the context, where sexual lust is depicted).
tn Heb “[at] a hand you gaze.” The term יָד (yad, “hand”) probably has the sense of “power, manhood” here, where it is used, as in Ugaritic, as a euphemism for the genitals. See HALOT 387 s.v. I יָד.

9.anchor: you go all the way to Sheol.
9.note: sn Israel’s devotion to her idols is inordinate, irrational, and self-destructive.

10.anchor: so you don’t collapse.
10.note: tn Heb “you do not grow weak.”

11.anchor: you are not afraid of me.
11.note: sn God’s patience with sinful Israel has caused them to think that they can sin with impunity and suffer no consequences.

12.anchor: I will denounce your so-called righteousness and your deeds,
12.note: tn Heb “I, I will declare your righteousness and your deeds.”

13.anchor: and will have access to
13.note: tn Heb “possess, own.” The point seems to be that he will have free access to God’s presence, as if God’s temple mount were his personal possession.

14.note: tn Since God is speaking throughout this context, perhaps we should emend the text to “and I say.” However, divine speech is introduced in v. 15.

15.anchor: and to encourage the discouraged.
15.note: tn Heb “to restore the lowly of spirit and to restore the heart of the crushed.”

16.anchor: for then man’s spirit would grow faint before me,
16.note: tn Heb “for a spirit from before me would be faint.”

17.anchor: yet they remained disobedient and stubborn.
17.note: tn Heb “and he walked [as an] apostate in the way of his heart.”

18.anchor: and I will once again console those who mourn.
18.note: tn Heb “and I will restore consolation to him, to his mourners.”

19.anchor: Complete prosperity
19.note: tn Heb “Peace, peace.” The repetition of the noun emphasizes degree.

2.anchor: who respect what I have to say.
2.note: tn Heb “to the humble and the lowly in spirit and the one who trembles at my words.”

3.anchor: they enjoy these disgusting practices.
3.note: tn Heb “their being [or “soul”] takes delight in their disgusting [things].”

4.anchor: They did evil before me;
4.note: tn Heb “that which is evil in my eyes.”

5.anchor: then we will witness your joy.”
5.note: tn Or “so that we might witness your joy.” The point of this statement is unclear.

8.note: tn Heb “land,” but here אֶרֶץ (’erets) stands metonymically for an organized nation (see the following line).

9.note: sn The rhetorical questions expect the answer, “Of course not!”

11.anchor: you will feed with joy from her milk-filled breasts.
11.note: tn Heb “you will slurp and refresh yourselves from her heavy breast.”sn Zion’s residents will benefit from and enjoy her great material prosperity. See v. 12.

12.anchor: You will nurse from her breast
12.note: tn The words “from her breast” are supplied in the translation for clarification (see v. 11).

13.anchor: As a mother consoles a child,
13.note: tn Heb “like a man whose mother comforts him.”

14.anchor: and his anger to his enemies.
14.note: tn Heb “and the hand of the Lord will be made known to his servants, and anger to his enemies.”

15.anchor: his battle cry, and his flaming arrows.
15.note: tn Heb “to cause to return with the rage of his anger, and his battle cry [or “rebuke”] with flames of fire.”

16.anchor: will kill many.
16.note: tn Heb “many are the slain of the Lord.”

17.anchor: “As for those who consecrate and ritually purify themselves so they can follow their leader and worship in the sacred orchards,
17.note: tn Heb “the ones who consecrate themselves and the ones who purify themselves toward the orchards [or “gardens”] after the one in the midst.” The precise meaning of the statement is unclear, though it is obvious that some form of idolatry is in view.

17.anchor: those who eat the flesh of pigs and other disgusting creatures, like mice
17.note: tn Heb “ones who eat the flesh of the pig and the disgusting thing and the mouse.”

17.anchor: – they will all be destroyed together,”
17.note: tn Heb “together they will come to an end.”

18.anchor: “I hate their deeds and thoughts! So I am coming
18.note: tc The Hebrew text reads literally “and I, their deeds and their thoughts, am coming.” The syntax here is very problematic, suggesting that the text may have suffered corruption. Some suggest that the words “their deeds and their thoughts” have been displaced from v. 17. This line presents two primary challenges. In the first place, the personal pronoun “I” has no verb after it. Most translations insert “know” for the sake of clarity (NASB, NRSV, NLT, ESV). The NIV has “I, because of their actions and their imaginations…” Since God’s “knowledge” of Israel’s sin occasions judgment, the verb “hate” is an option as well (see above translation). The feminine form of the next verb (בָּאָה, ba’ah) could be understood in one of two ways. One could provide an implied noun “time” (עֵת, ’et) and render the next line “the time is coming/has come” (NASB, ESV). One could also emend the feminine verb to the masculine בָּא (ba’) and have the “I” at the beginning of the line govern this verb as well (for the Lord is speaking here): “I am coming” (cf. NIV, NCV, NRSV, TEV, NLT).

18.anchor: to gather all the nations and ethnic groups;
18.note: tn Heb “and the tongues”; KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV “and tongues.”

19.anchor: I will perform a mighty act among them
19.note: tn Heb “and I will set a sign among them.” The precise meaning of this statement is unclear. Elsewhere “to set a sign” means “perform a mighty act” (Ps 78:43; Jer 32:20), “make [someone] an object lesson” (Ezek 14:8), and “erect a [literal] standard” (Ps 74:4).

19.anchor: and then send some of those who remain to the nations – to Tarshish, Pul,
19.note: tn Some prefer to read “Put” (i.e., Libya).

19.anchor: Lud
19.note: sn That is, Lydia (in Asia Minor).

19.anchor: (known for its archers
19.note: tn Heb “drawers of the bow” (KJV and ASV both similar).

19.anchor: ), Tubal, Javan,
19.note: sn Javan is generally identified today as Greece (so NIV, NCV, NLT).

19.anchor: and to the distant coastlands
19.note: tn Or “islands” (NIV).

20.anchor: They will bring back all your countrymen
20.note: tn Heb “brothers” (so NIV); NCV “fellow Israelites.”

20.anchor: . They will bring them
20.note: tn The words “they will bring them” are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.

20.anchor: on horses, in chariots, in wagons, on mules, and on camels
20.note: tn The precise meaning of this word is uncertain. Some suggest it refers to “chariots.” See HALOT 498 s.v. *כִּרְכָּרָה.

23.anchor: From one month
23.note: tn Heb “new moon.” The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on “in the future” in 2:2.

23.anchor: to the next and from one Sabbath to the next, all people
23.note: tn Heb “all flesh” (so KJV, ASV, NRSV); NAB, NASB, NIV “all mankind”; NLT “All humanity.”

23.anchor: will come to worship me,”
23.note: tn Or “bow down before” (NASB).

24.anchor: “They will go out and observe the corpses of those who rebelled against me, for the maggots that eat them will not die,
24.note: tn Heb “for their worm will not die.”

24.anchor: and the fire that consumes them will not die out.
24.note: tn Heb “and their fire will not be extinguished.”

24.anchor: All people will find the sight abhorrent.”
24.note: tn Heb “and they will be an abhorrence to all flesh.”sn This verse depicts a huge mass burial site where the seemingly endless pile of maggot-infested corpses are being burned.

1.note: tn Heb “the excavation of the hole.”
sn The “rock” and “quarry” refer here to Abraham and Sarah, the progenitors of the nation.

2.note: tn “Bless” may here carry the sense of “endue with potency, reproductive power.” See Gen 1:28.
tn Heb “and I made him numerous.”

3.anchor: Happiness and joy will be restored to
3.note: tn Heb “found in” (so NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV).

4.anchor: I will make my justice a light to the nations.
4.note: tn Heb “and my justice for a light to the nations I will cause to rest.”

5.anchor: they wait in anticipation for the revelation of my power.
5.note: tn Heb “for my arm” (so NIV, NRSV).

6.note: tn Heb “my righteousness [or “vindication”].”
tn Heb “will not be shattered [or “dismayed”].”

7.anchor: you people who are aware of my law!
7.note: tn Heb “people (who have) my law in their heart.”

8.note: tn Heb “my vindication”; many English versions “my righteousness”; NRSV, TEV “my deliverance”; CEV “my victory.”

9.note: tn The words “did you not” are understood by ellipsis (note the preceding line). The rhetorical questions here and in v. 10 expect the answer, “Yes, you certainly did!”

tn Hebrew תַּנִּין (tannin) is another name for the symbolic sea monster. See the note at 27:1. In this context the sea creature represents Egypt. See the note on the title “Proud One” earlier in this verse.

10.anchor: so those delivered from bondage
10.note: tn Heb “the redeemed” (so ASV, NASB, NIV, NRSV); KJV “the ransomed.”

11.anchor: grief and suffering will disappear.
11.note: tn Heb “grief and groaning will flee.”

12.anchor: of mere human beings who are as short-lived as grass?
12.note: tn Heb “Who are you that you are afraid of man who dies, and of the son of man who [as] grass is given up?” The feminine singular forms should probably be emended to the masculine singular (see v. 13). They have probably been influenced by the construction אַתְּ־הִיא (’at-hi’) in vv. 9-10.

13.anchor: Where is the anger of the oppressor?
13.note: tn The question anticipates the answer, “Ready to disappear!” See v. 14.

14.note: tn Heb “he will not lack his bread.”

16.anchor: to say to Zion, ‘You are my people.’”
16.note: tn The infinitives in v. 16b are most naturally understood as indicating the purpose of the divine actions described in v. 16a. The relationship of the third infinitive to the commission is clear enough – the Lord has made the addressee (his special servant?) his spokesman so that the latter might speak encouraging words to those in Zion. But how do the first two infinitives relate? The text seems to indicate that the Lord has commissioned the addressee so that the latter might create the universe! Perhaps creation imagery is employed metaphorically here to refer to the transformation that Jerusalem will experience (see 65:17-18).

17.anchor: the goblet full of intoxicating wine.
17.note: tn Heb “the goblet, the cup [that causes] staggering, you drank, you drained.”

19.anchor: But who consoles you?
19.note: tc The Hebrew text has אֲנַחֲמֵךְ (’anakhamekh), a first person form, but the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa reads correctly יִנַחֲמֵךְ (yinakhamekh), a third person form.

20.anchor: by the battle cry of your God.
20.note: tn Heb “those who are full of the anger of the Lord, the shout [or “rebuke”] of your God.”

22.anchor: the goblet full of my anger.
22.note: tn Heb “the goblet of the cup of my anger.”

23.anchor: I will put it into the hand of your tormentors
23.note: tn That is, to make them drink it.
